paragraphs.text: Public attention on Harry and Meghan, the Duke and Duchess of Sussex, is intense and often ambivalent. People worldwide watch to see whether the couple will thrive or merely survive. Critics predict divorce, debt, or failure, while admirers wonder if they will remake royal life. Since their move to California in 2020, there has been constant speculation that distance from Britain would undo their marriage, but so far those doomsday predictions have not come true.

Much of the scrutiny follows the Sussexes’ search for publicity. Meghan regularly posts on social media, appears at American events, and gives interviews that are examined for new revelations. She has turned her marriage and persona into a public brand, promoting a version of her past and present that critics say rewrites facts. Her careful control of narrative has helped her build global fame and commercial opportunities.

Harry has struggled to protect his princely status while also trading on it. He has not acknowledged the harm caused by his memoir Spare and by provocative interviews, and he is praised for charity work but criticized when he earns large fees for his name. My earlier book, Revenge, argued that Meghan abandoned old ties to pursue fame and manipulated events to shape her story; that arc reached a peak with her 2021 interview with Oprah. Since then the couple have often appeared separately, joining only when a joint appearance serves their publicity.

Family turmoil and scandals have weakened the monarchy and raised stakes for the Sussexes. Revelations about Prince Andrew led to his public disgrace, an action that shocked Harry and showed how quickly royal standing can be removed. Harry fears that his brother, the future king, could strip the Sussexes of titles and bar them from Britain. With the royal family under strain, 2026 is presented as a make-or-break year. The book begins in the week of Queen Elizabeth II’s death in September 2022 and looks again at these tensions and their consequences.

In the countdown to the event, Ella Robertson read Meghan Markle’s proposed speech on the Autocue and reacted: “She’s fucked it.” Instead of sticking to the agreed theme about One Young World, Meghan’s draft focused on herself, saying “I” nearly fifty times in ten minutes. On 5 September 2022 Meghan was due to speak to 2,300 delegates at One Young World’s conference in Manchester, a global forum that brings young leaders together each year to discuss major problems. Ella and her mother Kate had invited her despite deep nerves about the likely fallout.

The Robertsons worried because ‘Megxit’ was still raw: Harry and Meghan had left the UK in March 2020 and their return from California generated intense attention. Buckingham Palace had said the Queen would not meet Harry; Charles and William were cold to the couple. The Sussexes’ short UK itinerary — Manchester, Düsseldorf to promote the Invictus Games, then London for WellChild — guaranteed press coverage, which made the visit risky for the conference.

Younger audiences admired the Sussexes, seeing Meghan as a victim of racism and Harry as wronged by his family. The Robertsons believed many of their delegates would welcome Meghan, and they hoped her presence would boost the event. Still, they demanded reassurances from the Sussexes’ staff that nothing explosive would distract the conference. James Holt, the Sussexes’ chief of staff, said he knew of “nothing coming up,” but did not mention Meghan’s podcast plans and an interview due in The Cut. Holt’s job, plainly, was to build Meghan’s celebrity as a humanitarian figure.

Logistics were settled in July: One Young World paid return flights for an eleven-person entourage. Meghan had agreed her short Manchester speech would promote women’s empowerment, avoid speaking about herself and avoid conspicuously expensive clothes. The Robertsons’ caution came from past experience: Meghan had worked with One Young World before. In 2014 Misan Harriman urged the Robertsons to book her for Dublin, predicting she would become a star. In 2016 at Ottawa, newly linked to Harry, Meghan pressed for top billing, pushed into high-profile photos, then left abruptly for a TV set without delivering her scheduled speech, upsetting her publicist and conference staff.

Negotiations for Meghan’s high-profile 2019 slot at the Royal Albert Hall were chaotic, with mixed messages from palace aides. One Young World agreed to fund a £200,000 scholarship to secure her appearance, but Prince Charles later ordered a cancellation because the date clashed with his visit to Japan. The compromise was a BBC-filmed round-table on feminism at Windsor where Meghan introduced Harry; she appeared brittle after Archie’s birth but professional. Public sympathy grew: 72 female MPs expressed solidarity, and prominent American figures praised her. With targeted briefings, Meghan was building a narrative of suffering and activism that appealed to young supporters.

By 2022 Meghan’s Los Angeles media team — including Beth Herlihy, Miranda Barbot, Ashley Hansen and PR contact Keleigh Thomas Morgan — ran a tight operation. Hansen described herself as central to the Sussexes’ PR strategy, monitored press coverage daily, and planned strict controls for Manchester: restricted ticketing, no journalists inside the hall, a ban on outside spectators, and limited photographer access. Meghan also attempted to secure a Vogue cover timed to Manchester but fell out with editor Edward Enninful. Hansen negotiated Sky TV to cut to Westminster so the couple’s appearance would coincide with the Conservative leadership result.

Security and travel added pressure. Harry fretted that the Home Office would not provide armed protection and said he would “risk assassination” rather than cancel. Extra police cover and a reserved rail carriage were arranged. After last touches by Charlotte Tilbury and a break at the Kimpton Clocktower Hotel, the couple went to Bridgewater Hall. They emerged to applause and some boos; Meghan, flanked by her publicist, took the stage in a red dress. She abandoned the billed theme of ethical leadership and instead praised One Young World for seeing “what I wanted to see fully in myself,” then spoke mainly about her own rise. Ella Robertson judged her “tone-deaf” and “toxic.” The audience clapped politely, and the Sussexes hurried away, leaving organizers offended by their apparent indifference.

The couple’s schedule continued. In Düsseldorf they were treated like heads of state and Harry enjoyed interviews; back in London at the WellChild awards he was warmly received. They returned to Frogmore Cottage as attention shifted elsewhere. On 7 September 2022 Edward Young, the Queen’s private secretary, was at Balmoral with the ailing monarch as plans for Operation London Bridge were put in motion. The next morning Harry and Meghan would confront the depth of the Royal Family’s distrust that had shadowed their visit.

They met on a blind date in July 2016, arranged while she worked at Wimbledon. At thirty-four, Meghan was single, about to lose her role on Suits, and had been planning to freeze her eggs. Landing Harry changed her prospects: she had left a Canadian boyfriend and stepped into a relationship that she later described as astonishing luck.

Meghan researched Harry and learned he was emotionally vulnerable. After meeting in Soho House she arranged a return visit and accepted his invitation to a safari in Botswana. There she listened as he recounted long-standing troubles, showing the patience that had eluded his previous partner, Cressida Bonas, who had left because of Harry’s paranoia. Within weeks they were committed. Palace officials, watching from the sidelines, grew suspicious and some muttered that Meghan had found a “meal ticket,” a notion that angered Harry.

Harry framed much of his unhappiness as the result of the royal hierarchy, saying the institution had controlled and imprisoned him and left him only the role of the unloved "spare." He was too drifting and inarticulate to explain fully to Meghan how the monarchy worked, and the Palace responded by preparing her for royal life before the wedding, following the Queen’s orders to coach her for the constraints of a constitutional monarchy.

That preparation included formal religious rites and practical briefings. The Archbishop of Canterbury oversaw Meghan’s baptism and confirmation in St James’s Chapel Royal, attended by the Queen and Prince Philip, though some observers doubted she took the vows seriously. Edward Lane Fox, Harry’s private secretary, gave her a thirty-point dossier outlining the family’s many patronages and the royal role as politically neutral unifier of British society. Samantha Cohen and other senior courtiers also explained the Queen’s need to avoid public opinion and maintain discretion. Despite these efforts, officials later felt Meghan did not absorb the lessons; Harry would later claim "Meghan wasn't given one minute of training," while others blamed lack of engagement on her.

A senior official who met Meghan monthly found her dominant and self-assured. He told her, "You’re not just marrying Harry Windsor, you’re marrying the British constitution," warning that royal life meant fences, constant observation, and strict limits. Meghan listened but often appeared uninterested. She sympathized with the Duchess of Windsor who left Britain after the Abdication, yet she also believed she could set her own terms rather than submit. The official came to think the marriage would be bumpy if she refused to conform.

Meghan’s view of the monarchy as, in effect, a set of celebrity roles rather than a historic institution deepened frictions. She spoke as if the Queen ran the Firm like a studio president and showed little interest in the web of traditions and unseen demands that sustained the monarchy. Tensions rose before the wedding when Meghan made decisions without consulting senior royals: the Queen later complained privately that Meghan’s dress was "too white" for a divorcee, and Meghan approached the Archbishop about the ceremony without first speaking to the Dean of Windsor. The Queen reprimanded Harry; he defended Meghan, aligning with her frustrations and further widening family divides.

After the wedding the gulf was more visible. Meghan excluded the monarch from her Vogue guest-editor list of fifteen "Game Changers," reinforcing the view that she had rebuffed the Queen’s attempts to bring her into the family. Her meeting with Thomas Woodcock at the College of Arms, where she sought a personal coat of arms, produced an awkward episode when she initially asked for a songbird depicted with its tongue cut out; she later agreed on a nightingale without the silencing motif.

Wedding day dramas reinforced suspicions about Meghan’s priorities and behavior. Thomas Markle, her father, admitted staging photographs and later suffered a heart attack; Meghan had told advisers she hoped he would not attend, and he was ultimately prevented from flying to London. Meghan’s solitary entrance down the aisle, evoking a Hollywood tableau, and her triumphant carriage ride won enormous public attention. The ceremony cost about 32 million pounds and confirmed her status as a global celebrity, welcomed by many in the crowd including Black Britons who identified with her.

Still, the celebrations also revealed social rifts. Meghan’s guest list favored Hollywood acquaintances and left out some of Harry’s oldest friends; palace staff noticed her disdain for rougher English habits and suspected she planned to return to California. Officials concluded that both sides had unrealistic expectations: Meghan refused limits and the family assumed she would naturally fit in. Within six months she was irritated at the low-key reaction to her official duties, feeling unrecognized compared with Hollywood. Her assertiveness grew, palace patience waned, and William and Kate became increasingly aggrieved, leaving the relationship between Meghan and the established family deeply strained.

Tea in the Cambridges' newly redecorated twenty-room Kensington apartment after the Sussexes' June 2018 honeymoon was meant to be a reconciliation. Instead it broke down into a sharp confrontation in which Meghan snapped, telling William to take his finger out of her face. The formal setting could not hide the anger and old resentments between the two couples.

Tension had begun well before the wedding. William had warned Harry that the relationship had moved too quickly, and Kate distrusted Meghan from the start. Meghan’s confident, American manner and career background clashed with the family’s expectations. She later told friends the Royal Family hated ambition; that view reinforced the sense on both sides that they came from different worlds.

Money and status widened the gap. Kensington staff noticed Meghan’s surprise at the limited pay and perks of royal life; she had expected a Hollywood-style elevation. Small cultural clashes became symbolic: Kate disapproved of Meghan’s jeans and recoiled when Meghan shared lip gloss. Meghan, who thought of herself as warm and open, said she had been a hugger and did not understand British reserve.

Meghan rejected some royal conventions and the hierarchy they represented. She refused to curtsy to Kate and mocked the formality of greetings. The clash of styles fed mutual contempt: Meghan saw Kate as out of touch, while the Cambridges viewed Meghan as unwilling to accept her role. Observers believed some people even suspected Meghan imagined herself more suited to be queen.

The most immediate flashpoint was the bridesmaid dress for Princess Charlotte. A Givenchy design had proved ill-fitting and left three-year-old Charlotte in tears. A tailor had to be summoned to alter the dresses. Text messages between Kate and Meghan after the episode showed tension: Meghan urged practical action; Kate replied that all the dresses should be remade. Kate, newly fragile after giving birth to Louis three weeks earlier, became tearful under the pressure.

Palace staff complained about Meghan’s manner. Meetings over gifts, opinions and communications turned into arguments. Senior aides described her as difficult, passively aggressive and manipulative. Meghan said she was not there to coddle people and was baffled by the indirect British way of speaking; a senior official observed that she did not grasp that the family spoke in code rather than plain language.

At the tea party the argument turned personal. Meghan listed grievances about Easter presents, fashion, and the bridesmaid dress. She then said Kate’s mood was down to hormones and called it baby brain. Kate and William reacted angrily; William told Meghan bluntly that ‘‘that’s not how things are done here’’ and jutted his finger at her. The confrontation felt like a final rupture.

Watching the scene, Harry felt years of grievance confirmed. He blamed his family’s negativity for a lifetime of hurt and saw the palace and the press as aligned against him and Meghan. He believed Kate feared Meghan as a rival and that public adoration of Kate hid insecurity and duty. The brothers left deeply estranged; the split between them was sealed.

Returning to Nottingham Cottage, Meghan and Harry were distraught. The cramped cottage, without luxuries Meghan mocked, contrasted with the Cambridges’ ornate apartment and reminded Harry of his secondary place. Senior courtiers felt he should have accepted his role as the spare, but he could not. After the tea the relationship between the brothers was broken and Meghan’s demeanor hardened; she became more determined that the family should recognize her importance and protect her path forward.

Before he met Meghan, Harry had a generally warm relationship with William and Kate, although he saw less of his brother after Prince George was born in 2013. At first Meghan’s arrival seemed to brighten everything and people at the palace noticed the change. Yet beneath that early optimism lay long-standing family strains that shaped how Harry saw his place in the family and how others treated him.

Harry grew up shadowed by loss and mixed attention from senior royals. The Queen took a clear, steady interest in William, teaching him at Windsor to behave publicly, to test friendships slowly, and to observe state business with aides like Christopher Geidt. By contrast, several insiders said the Queen could be patronizing toward Harry. Where William was carefully guided as heir, Harry received less focused support from the institution.

The pattern of uneven parenting ran through earlier generations. Prince Philip’s difficult childhood and Charles’s own distance produced a family culture in which Charles often avoided confrontation and was sometimes absentee as a father. Rumors and a comment recounted by Harry about paternity fed more insecurity. Diana, strong-willed and emotionally direct, did not share many of Charles’s tastes and, according to witnesses, treated Harry as the needier son, deepening his sense of difference from William.

Harry’s life was insulated and privileged: chauffeurs, bodyguards, staff who handled daily chores, and little ordinary independence. He also rebelled, partying in London clubs, drinking heavily, and using drugs; a tabloid expose when he was seventeen revealed cocaine use. The palace refused to cover up his behavior, a decision that angered Harry because he felt the institution protected William’s reputation at his expense. The label of the ‘‘spare’’ — a perpetual backup to the heir — increasingly felt intolerable to him.

Harry’s relationship with Charles grew strained for personal reasons as well as symbolic ones. He blamed his father for failures around Diana and for emotional distance, citing moments such as Charles’s failure to embrace him after Diana’s death and Charles’s later marriage to Camilla as signs of betrayal. Service in the army and deployment to Afghanistan offered Harry a sense of belonging and purpose; after a high-profile evacuation from the war zone he struggled with symptoms he later described as post-traumatic stress. William, according to Harry, urged him to seek help, and the brothers later founded the Heads Together mental health campaign in 2016.

By the time William married Kate and the Cambridges gained staff, cars, and a larger home, Harry felt increasingly sidelined. He complained of having ‘‘no money, no status, no decent home and no job’’ and asked for a separate role or household; palace officials declined. Small disputes at Kensington Palace — over parking, sunlight, and space — took on outsized meaning. Harry believed Kate and William had tightened his margins, and he told William that meeting Meghan felt like finding a connection to Diana. William’s reply, reserved and uncertain, showed the widening distance between them.

In the weeks after the tea party incident, Meghan fed Harry's suspicions and encouraged him to challenge his family. She had publicly criticized members of her own family and refused to defend her father, and she urged Harry to view his relatives and the palace as hostile and racist. Meghan compared the palace's treatment of her to the media treatment of Diana, which stirred Harry's long-standing bitterness about his mother's death and his belief that the press had chased her to death.

Harry clung to the belief that Diana had been victimized and manipulated by journalists, while largely ignoring evidence that she often used the media herself. The Panorama scandal with Martin Bashir and her close ties to tabloid reporters showed Diana deliberately shaping stories and attacking Charles. Her relationship with Dodi Fayed and publicity plans in the summer of 1997, including calls to editors about staged photos, illustrated how she courted attention even as she presented herself as a victim.

Harry preferred sympathetic portrayals of his mother, such as those on Netflix, over the complex reality. He distrusted the newspapers and failed to recognize how the monarchy depended on carefully managed relationships with selected journalists to promote royal work and bury damaging stories. He equated palace media operations with deliberate distortion rather than routine public relations.

Palace officials began to see Meghan as a similar risk to Diana. Camilla later thought Harry had been "radicalised by Meghan." Photographers like Arthur Edwards noticed a change in Harry after he met Meghan: where he used to be warm and open, he became guarded and distant, which signaled a deeper rupture in his public behavior.

Meghan brought a professional, image-driven approach from her life near Hollywood. She closed her commercial website when told to do so, then grew resentful of restrictions on commercial activity and public access. She recorded private moments, like Harry's proposal announcement, and immediately resumed contact with agents in Los Angeles to prepare a possible return to California after marriage.

Tensions inside the royal household grew through 2018. By September, the Queen's private secretary Edward Young was frustrated with Harry's repeated demands and his sense of exclusion. The Sussexes felt cut off from family decision-making and believed palace officials were keeping them from the Queen, which increased resentment on both sides.

Practical disputes multiplied. The Sussexes rejected Nottingham Cottage and were offered Frogmore cottages that needed heavy refurbishment. Harry refused to share an office with William and interpreted offers of space at Buckingham Palace as a snub. Preparations for the October 2018 Australia and New Zealand tour were strained. Meghan announced her pregnancy at Princess Eugenie's wedding, which shifted media attention and heightened tensions.

On the eve of the tour two staffers made formal complaints to Jason Knauf about Meghan's behavior, saying they felt traumatized by abrupt rudeness and humiliations. Harry urged Knauf to ignore the complaints and protect Meghan, even suggesting NDAs. Knauf later composed a memorandum to Simon Case describing repeated reports of bullying and manipulation by the duchess, yet he delayed sending it. Harry's lawyers later denied he had interfered.

The tour itself brought large crowds and positive press in Australia, which Harry read as proof that Meghan was a global star and that the palace was jealous. Meghan, however, did not always grasp that public enthusiasm was often for the royal institution as much as for her. Incidents such as Meghan forbidding Harry to meet David Beckham at an Invictus event fed reports of internal conflict and soured relations with staff and other guests.

Back in Britain, reports of staff complaints continued. Knauf forwarded his concerns to Simon Case on October 26, and human resources at Clarence House judged the situation serious. Staff departures followed, including Melissa Toubati resigning after six months. The Sussexes returned to a cool reception in London. A heated argument between William and Harry at Nottingham Cottage turned violent in Harry's account; he called his therapist afterward.

The public row with Meghan's father, Thomas Markle, added to the strain. Meghan had written him a letter via her manager and asked Knauf to help shape narrative material for a book project. Knauf later spoke to Omid Scobie about the couple for the book that became Finding Freedom, though Meghan later said she had not asked him to do so. Christmas at Sandringham showed a polite public front while private frostiness remained, and Meghan confided to friends that palace rules stifled her and left her feeling constrained.

By January 2019 Meghan reached a breaking point and later said she had felt suicidal. The People magazine story in February 2019, based on interviews with friends and leaks about her letter to her father, inflamed the situation. The global reaction painted Meghan as having betrayed her father. Knauf refused to answer media inquiries, and palace officials saw Harry as Meghan's ally in attacking the institution. Thomas Markle called his daughter controlling and published parts of her letter. Supporters of the Sussexes, the so-called "Sussex Squad," then condemned journalists as sexist and racist, and the conflict between the couple and the media became openly bitter.

Meghan felt a turning point after the wedding. She had long wanted media attention and, once married to Harry, found herself exercising power by shaping his relationships with the Palace, his family, and Britain. She convinced Harry that Palace intrigue targeted them both and suggested that leaving Britain might be the only cure for their unhappiness. Later she reflected it was "bittersweet" and implied that if officials had met her demands she would not have returned to California.

Harry became consumed by what he called a toxic British media. He spent hours online reading attacks on Meghan and believed the tabloids and trolls were bent on destroying their lives. Advised not to read the press, he nevertheless grew convinced his family was complicit in a campaign against the Sussexes, accusing Palace officials and, possibly, William and Kate of feeding the antagonism. He believed he and Meghan were victims of institutional gaslighting and feared his mother’s fate could be repeated.

In February 2019, six weeks before Archie was born, Meghan flew to New York for a baby shower hosted by Serena Williams in a luxury suite at The Mark Hotel. The event signaled her embrace of Hollywood glamour and helped set the stage for a return to Los Angeles. On her agent’s advice she launched a new website to promote Brand Sussex while claiming publicly that she didn’t read social media. Privately she negotiated her return to California and pitched to Hollywood producers, even as Harry and the Palace prepared separate offices for the Sussexes.

Archie was born on May 6 at the Portland Hospital in London. The limited information released sparked conspiracy theories that Archie was a surrogate baby. Meghan also broke royal convention by refusing to allow photos of Archie with the Queen and Prince Philip to be released; Palace officials concluded she had not been truthful about the reasons. Initially Meghan said no picture had been requested and later framed decisions about Archie’s title and protection as evidence of racial bias, claims that were inaccurate. At birth Archie held the traditional courtesy title Lord Dumbarton.

Behind the scenes, the first photographs of Archie were negotiated as an exclusive to CBS, a deal tied to later broadcast interviews. Observers believed Meghan withheld images because British papers would not pay; the couple used American media relationships to monetise and publicise their story. Harry agreed to participate in an Apple TV series called Mental Wellness with Oprah Winfrey, positioning the couple for a high-profile move to the United States.

By mid-2019 William’s worry that Meghan was a divisive influence seemed confirmed. Harry cut off old friends and even changed his phone number. The Sussex household grew chaotic, staff were exhausted, and several aides resigned. Insiders said Harry had become fixed on revenge, mirroring Meghan’s intensity. He told a narrative of Meghan in tears and of poisonous tabloids, while Meghan maintained she did not read the stories; observers noted ambiguity about the authenticity of some public displays of emotion.

A key turning point was a meeting organized by Elton John in the South of France, where Harry was introduced to the lawyer David Sherborne. Sherborne had worked on Leveson material and won a major case against Mirror Group, in which the judge described unlawful practices as "extensive and habitual." Sherborne argued that many papers, including News UK, had paid private investigators and could be sued for damages. He offered contingency arrangements and an insurance plan so Harry would face little financial risk, presenting litigation as a way to find out if he had been hacked and to punish the tabloids.

Sherborne’s pitch appealed to Harry’s anger and sense of grievance. He embraced a campaign of legal claims against Mirror Group and News UK while Meghan moved ahead with her own case against the Mail on Sunday for publishing parts of a letter from her father. Sherborne promised a staggered legal assault and identified past rulings and Leveson material to support big claims. Harry began to blame the media not only for intrusion but for his past mistakes and for blocking his attempts at redress, including an alleged secret agreement between the Palace and News Group that he said had left him excluded from a settlement.

Back in London, Harry consulted Gerrard Tyrrell of Harbottle & Lewis, who advised against suing News Group. On Sherborne’s advice the couple distrusted Tyrrell, claiming he took directions from Clarence House, and they replaced him with more aggressive lawyers, including Schillings for Meghan. Harry said he had spoken to William and then to the Queen, and believed he had permission to proceed; Palace officials were not formally consulted on the litigation timetable. The Sussexes prepared to make attacking the media the opening act of their break with Britain, planning to broadcast their declaration in South Africa.

On the eve of a ten day official tour of South Africa, Beth Herlihy, a private secretary at Kensington Palace, told colleagues she thought Meghan was suffering from the baby blues and was ‘‘very unhappy.’’ Palace staff had spent months trying to meet the Sussexes changing demands. Meghan insisted that four month old Archie join the tour, despite having refused a recent Balmoral visit for the same reason. Some officials began to suspect that Harry planned to use the trip to promote a personal agenda rather than Britain s reputation.

Publicly the first days of the tour were a triumph. Journalists were tightly managed and Meghan gave polished speeches presenting herself as a mother, feminist and campaigner. Privately the couple ignored palace advice and the Sussexes team was in turmoil: staff members were in tears, Jason Knauf and the human resources director resigned, and courtiers described Meghan s behavior as difficult. Unseen by the media, Harry prepared a forceful public statement drafted by lawyers in London.

Harry published a post on the Sussexes official website attacking the British press for ‘‘relentless propaganda’’ and saying there was a human cost when press stories were knowingly false. He called the behavior bullying, warned it destroyed people, and invoked the death of his mother, saying he feared history repeating itself as Meghan became commodified. Soon after, lawyers filed a High Court claim against the Mail on Sunday, accusing it of stoking a dispute with Meghan s father and exploiting him. The headlines called the actions ill advised and the mood on the tour turned sour.

At the same time the couple were filmed for an ITV documentary by Tom Bradby. Rather than focus on South African issues, the interviews became a platform for Harry and Meghan s grievances. Harry described the lasting pain of his mother s death and said his relationship with William was fractured, that they were ‘‘on different paths at the moment.’’ Meghan spoke emotionally about feeling vulnerable in pregnancy and struggling with tabloids, saying she was not okay and that surviving was not enough, she wanted to thrive. The couple announced they needed to leave to protect their family and returned to Britain. Two years later Meghan would say she had been silenced within the institution.

Harry was summoned to Buckingham Palace to meet the Queen s and Prince of Wales private secretaries, Clive Alderton and Edward Young. Alderton, a career diplomat who had worked for Charles, was seen as a traditional, steady courtier who avoided challenging his employer. Young, a former corporate communications head and deputy to Christopher Geidt, was viewed by insiders as cautious and less commanding. After Geidt s exit, the royal households were run by courtiers guarding their own roles and often in tension with each other.

On media strategy the two private secretaries agreed: Harry should not sue News UK and should try to delay the ITV broadcast to avoid overshadowing the Cambridges Pakistan tour. Harry ignored their advice. ITV aired the Bradby program during the Cambridges visit, which many judged to have undermined that trip and to have damaged the Sussexes standing within the royal family. An important documentary promoting another royal was also hurt by the timing. The Sussexes lost the benefit of any doubt.

Afterwards Hillary Clinton visited Meghan at Frogmore House and expressed sympathy, saying Meghan s treatment was heart breaking and wrong. Meghan s publicist found the couple temporary refuge on Vancouver Island, at a waterfront house called Mille Fleurs arranged by the music producer David Foster. They moved there with Canadian protection officers and announced they would not spend Christmas at Sandringham, that they planned a presence in a Commonwealth country, and that Meghan would resume business activities from that time zone. From Vancouver she kept close contact with her business manager, lawyer and publicist as the couple began preparing their final move to Los Angeles.

Harry and Meghan wanted status, money, and protection from the Royal Family, and within eighteen months of their wedding they were negotiating an exit. At Meghan s direction, Harry wrote an email to his father setting out their demands, but held it back fearing a Christmas leak. Unknown to him, a member of his staff had passed the draft to a tabloid journalist. The paper ultimately did not publish the scoop, but the episode presaged a widening split between the couple and the institution they served.

Charles and the Queen already knew from phone calls that Harry wanted to disengage from royal service. Among his proposals was the suggestion that they would be willing to relinquish their Sussex titles if necessary, though Harry did not acknowledge that such titles could probably only be removed by an act of Parliament. The Queen told him to speak only to Charles. Charles and William agreed they would not accede to Harry s demands, and all three senior royals sought advice from their private secretaries, above all the Queen s private secretary, Edward Young.

Young, Clive Alderton (Charles s secretary), and Simon Case (William s secretary) were cautious, loyal courtiers who avoided taking bold initiatives. They had been aware of complaints about Meghan s treatment of staff but had taken no strong action, preferring not to inflame the situation. Their role was to protect the Crown, not to side with an individual royal, and they advised in line with what they believed the Queen and the monarchy needed.

Just before Christmas 2019 officials in Canada indicated that the Sussexes intended to stay in North America and not return to Britain permanently. The Queen was unsurprised. On her orders, no photograph of the Sussexes was placed beside her for the Christmas broadcast; watching in Vancouver, Harry was shocked by the consequences of his choices. Discussions were scheduled for January, and Harry expected to see the Queen at Sandringham to press for a compromise that would allow the couple a semi exile.

Harry believed the Queen understood and would support them, but those closest to her were suspicious of Meghan and felt Harry had been influenced by his wife. At the last minute Harry was told the Sandringham visit was canceled. Furious on a nine hour flight, he blamed the three private secretaries. In his later memoir he attacked them with nicknames and harsh caricatures, yet he misunderstood that their loyalty was to the monarchy itself, not to him personally.

After landing, Harry told Young he was struggling with his mental health and wanted to make a public statement and return to Canada. Young advised patience and warned not to upset the Queen. The following morning the Sun published Harry s emailed proposals. On January 8 the couple issued a public statement that they would be stepping back as full time senior royals and split their time between Britain and North America to carve out a new role, a rupture quickly dubbed Megxit.

The private secretaries met and prepared options for Harry s future, ranging from staying as working royals to full severance with loss of security. The Queen sympathized with the idea of making space for a spare, but she would not allow the Sussexes to exploit royal status for profit. Meghan s commercial plans, supported by her advisers in Los Angeles, included registering consumer items to be sold under the Sussex Royal brand. The Palace made clear that royal titles could not be used for private income.

Meghan returned to Vancouver intending to join the Sandringham summit by video, but Young excluded her because of concern she might record or monetize the talks. She denounced the decision as misogynistic. At Sandringham the family meeting was tense. Accounts differ, but the Queen made the core decision: you are either in or you are out. The outcome was option five, ordering Harry to give up royal appointments and the HRH style and forbidding either of them to use their titles for commercial gain, while publicly acknowledging they would remain family and expressing hope they would build a peaceful life.

Harry left Sandringham furious after Kensington Palace issued a rebuttal to a newspaper story in both his and William s names, a statement he said he had not approved. He blamed William and Kate, seeing primogeniture and brotherly spite behind his loss of support, commands, and funding. Attempts to sway him back, including a brief meeting with the prime minister, failed. Back on Vancouver Island the couple felt isolated and treated as irrelevant. Public reaction was split: some saw ingratitude and self destruction, others, including some Black commentators, cited racism as a key reason for Meghan s decision to leave.

Heathrow’s Windsor Lounge offered privacy and luxury to the very wealthy and became a pivotal place for Prince Harry in January 2020 as he waited to return to Vancouver. In the weeks after the Sandringham talks, Meghan moved quickly to secure commercial deals. Her Los Angeles business manager and agent promised a Netflix contract and other opportunities, and she urged Harry to monetise his anger. She believed betrayal made a powerful story for television and memoirs, and she arranged for Jennifer Rudolph Walsh at William Morris Endeavor to act as Harry’s literary agent, promoting a brutal exposé of royal life.

Meghan, who criticised her father for giving interviews, was herself preparing high-profile media appearances with Vogue, People, Oprah Winfrey and Netflix. She also advanced plans for Archewell, a California organisation meant to produce influential narratives. The couple’s later claim they arrived in America penniless ignored several financial facts: Harry had inherited over 10 million pounds, Meghan had savings of about 2 million dollars, and their agents promised large advances.

Living on Vancouver Island, Harry hesitated to move to Los Angeles, fearing a final break would expose his earlier promises to the Crown as empty. They did return to London on March 5 to stage a public farewell. Meghan choreographed photo opportunities, Harry made an emotional speech to the Royal Marines, and the couple endured a tense appearance with the Cambridges at the Commonwealth Day Service. The Royal Family’s coolness deepened their sense of grievance, a strain their authorised biographer later described as avoidable.

Meghan made a prepared public lament to the press about sacrificing her life for the family, which a friendly publicist fed to the Daily Mail. Some journalists and commentators echoed Meghan’s version, accusing the family of racism and failed attempts at reconciliation; others accused Meghan of exploiting the royals for fame. Back in Canada, the government removed 24-hour taxpayer-funded police protection around their home, and the Palace reminded Harry that his role as a working royal would end on March 31, with instructions to close the SussexRoyal Instagram account.

In March, Jennifer Rudolph Walsh advised that Penguin Random House in New York would likely make the best bid for Harry’s autobiography. The publisher suggested that three bestselling books could net the couple over 20 million dollars, with the first advance around 3 million. This prospect pushed the Sussexes toward an early move to California to begin work on projects and to seek the income required to sustain their new life.

Meghan sought a loan of a house and a private jet through Los Angeles contacts. Tyler Perry answered their appeal and on March 14, just before widespread Covid travel restrictions, he flew the Sussexes and their possessions to Los Angeles on his private jet. Harry recorded a video calling the trip the “Freedom Flight,” framing the move as escape from the abuse directed at his wife and son. Meghan and Harry both understood that filmed, private material and intimate access would increase their commercial value to buyers like Netflix.

They settled first in Tyler Perry’s Beverly Ridge home and then bought a Mediterranean-style mansion in Montecito. The property had been reduced from $34 million and the Sussexes paid the owner $5 million cash plus a $9.5 million mortgage. Their lifestyle, staff costs and security would require an income of about 3 million dollars a year, contradicting later claims that they had arrived in California without money.

The couple announced Archewell would be rebranded sussex.com and used royal styling, including the coat of arms. Meghan expected social status and charitable invitations in California and planned a privileged life for Archie. On their first morning in Montecito, Meghan suffered a miscarriage. Harry publicly blamed the Mail on Sunday for contributing to the stress and pursued legal action against the paper; the chapter notes that miscarriages are common and mentions Meghan’s past pre-eclampsia as additional context.

Harry experienced loneliness and friction with his family. He called the Queen and demanded apologies and money, conversations that became heated, and the Queen suggested he speak to his father, who was often unavailable. To run their projects in California, the Sussexes recruited trusted staff, including James Holt from their palace household, to help establish Archewell and support their work.

Penguin Random House appointed J.R. Moehringer, an experienced ghostwriter, to work with Harry. Moehringer, known for Open and The Tender Bar, met Harry and Meghan over Zoom during Covid and was briefed to chronicle an intimate, honest account. Harry used the writer as a confessor and wanted the book to rebut every published lie and settle scores. Meghan encouraged full candor, while Moehringer and editors in New York envisioned a narrative portraying Harry as a man trapped by family trauma and public life.

Differences emerged between Harry’s expectation of obedience and Moehringer’s independence, but the publisher sought a blockbuster. The manuscript included intimate and sensational material that would sell widely: sexual anecdotes and repeated references to Harry’s genitalia, vivid stories of losing his virginity and embarrassing others, confessions of drug use, and raw attacks on family members, especially Camilla and Charles. Editors prioritized salability, and the finished book promised to deliver sex, drugs, and explosive revelations about the Royal Family.

During the Covid lockdown Netflix’s business surged and its content chiefs, including Ted Sarandos, saw the Sussexes as a guaranteed hit. Sarandos agreed to a five-series, five-year deal after Brandon Riegg and others judged the couple would draw huge audiences. It surprised some that Harry would partner with Netflix, the maker of The Crown, which had dramatized his family’s story in ways he said he preferred. Meghan insisted their first big appearance be the Oprah interview in the UK before Netflix released its series, and as rumors of a $100 million contract spread the couple became a high-value commodity and Meghan’s public brand grew stronger.

Meghan shaped a public image that mixed compassion, victimhood, racism, feminism, motherhood and moral purpose. In the run-up to the 2020 U.S. election the Sussexes openly supported Joe Biden and spoke against Trump, praising changes in Biden’s language about gender. Meghan met California governor Gavin Newsom, and the couple used their Time 100 recognition to speak directly to Americans from their Montecito garden, urging compassion and warning against hate speech and misinformation. Earlier incidents, like Meghan’s apparent refusal to join Harry for the state dinner with Donald Trump and Harry’s cold treatment of Ivanka, fed public friction; Trump said he was not a fan of Meghan.

Meghan had framed herself as a victim of racism since a 2015 Elle piece, and the murder of George Floyd intensified her public alignment with Black Lives Matter. She called the movement a necessary, inspirational reckoning and Harry echoed her concerns, describing British institutions as affected by endemic structural racism. The couple backed BLM arguments about white privilege but pulled back from some public criticism when the movement’s calls to remove statues and censor books became controversial. As they prepared for Oprah and for their Netflix work, Meghan shaped a narrative that centered her experience of racism for a global audience.

Behind the rhetoric, Meghan lacked proven skills as a producer or long-form storyteller. She told professionals the Netflix series would be "our love story," but those who would work with her noted her naivety about narrative and filmmaking. Critics also targeted her children’s book The Bench as weak and accused her repeatedly of borrowing ideas, echoing how her website, The Tig, had mirrored influencer formulas. Like many actors, she relied on others to craft words; she showed little patience for admitting creative limits. Harry largely watched as Meghan chose four producers, all women, and key collaborators including Giona Jefferson.

Meghan selected Garrett Bradley as director, attracted by Bradley’s focus on Black women, social justice, and empathetic, observational filmmaking. Bradley described her method as non‑agenda, listening first and letting prolonged filming create the script from images and performances, with access to intimate spaces. That approach clashed with Meghan’s need for control: she expected carefully staged, polished presentation and would not allow unscripted filming in private areas. Filming began in autumn 2020 but stopped within weeks; Bradley’s footage was called "chaotic," and Meghan began searching for another collaborator. The project then suffered delays as indecision and a desire to tightly manage the Sussexes’ public image slowed progress.

At the end of 2020 Harry and Meghan were confident but consumed by legal fights with British tabloids. Their lawyer, David Sherborne, reported progress: Harry’s claims against the Mirror and the Sun were moving toward trial, and they expected Meghan to win her case against the Mail on Sunday. The couple framed their campaign as a fight against a press industry that treated them as “currency for an economy of click‑bait and distortion.”

The Sussexes accused Palace staff, including Jason Knauf, of leaving Meghan exposed to relentless, inaccurate stories. Publicly they positioned themselves as honorable victims pulled apart for profit; privately, however, cooperation with sympathetic authors complicated that stance. In August 2020 Omid Scobie’s Finding Freedom presented a narrative that echoed the Sussexes’ view: Palace aides were sexist and racist, William and Kate were blamed for rifts, and Thomas Markle was portrayed negatively.

Meghan denied assisting Scobie but evidence contradicted that denial. Jason Knauf briefed Scobie for two hours and Meghan’s team made introductions; Meghan also told friends about a private letter to her father that later appeared in a magazine. Her legal team argued judges should avoid a trial to protect her privacy and claimed the tabloids had printed “hundreds of thousands of inaccurate articles.”

Four Kensington Palace aides offered to testify about Meghan’s role in preparing the Scobie material and the letter. Nonetheless, Mr. Justice Warby ruled that Meghan’s relationship with her father was private and that evidence contradicting her statements was irrelevant, granting her victory without a trial. The judgment prevented fuller public scrutiny of the competing accounts.

The ruling produced jubilation in Montecito. Meghan celebrated the legal win as justice for privacy and reliable journalism. At the same time the couple prepared a broad commercial program: business registrations in California and Delaware anticipated documentaries, books, and other ventures. Netflix became central to that plan.

Meghan chose director Liz Garbus for a Netflix series that would mix the Sussexes’ love story with accusations against the Royal Family and critiques of the British press and empire. She also developed an animated children’s project called Pearl; that pitch drew a claim from an author whose books had similar themes. Netflix later cancelled the animation, saying the pitch failed to tell a visual story.

Planning for a global media moment intensified around a major television interview with Oprah Winfrey. Meghan and Harry wanted to present a single, powerful narrative of mistreatment, racism, and lack of institutional protection. Meghan rehearsed how she would appear on camera and chose bullying and racism as the interview’s central themes, knowing those topics would strongly resonate in the U.S.

In the televised interview Meghan said there had been “concerns” and “conversations” about how dark her son Archie’s skin might be, and she declined to identify who made those remarks. She also said she contemplated suicide during pregnancy and that Palace officials denied her request for help. Harry described feeling in peril and said silence was no longer an option; he added the couple had no choice but to speak publicly.

Harry’s version of the race conversation differed: he said there had been only one awkward early discussion about what their children might look like, before marriage, and that Meghan was not present. Media attention focused on the most damaging claims and the program drew 49 million viewers. Shortly before the broadcast a Knauf memorandum about Meghan’s alleged bullying of staff was leaked to The Times, producing a fierce backlash and accusations that the Palace had retaliated.

The interview prompted a global debate. Supporters praised the Sussexes; critics accused them of grievance and one‑sided storytelling. The Palace responded that “recollections may vary.” Charles privately wrote to Meghan denying family racism; Meghan replied and later identified the person privately, though public statements would later soften that identification to “unconscious bias.” The Sussexes said the Oprah program would be their last word on the rift, even as they proceeded with a Netflix series and other projects.

Liz Garbus assembled a tightly controlled film crew for the Sussexes’ project. Applicants were vetted for loyalty by checking social media and interviewed about discretion. Those hired in November 2021 signed strict non-disclosure agreements and followed rules: never speak to the couple unless directly addressed, never use their names, and address Meghan as “Ma’am” and Harry as “Sir.” Filming was scheduled to begin in December. Garbus recorded interviews over ten days in a rented house, with eight-hour sessions and cameras sometimes hidden behind black screens so the crew saw only monitors. No Archewell staff appeared on set.

Garbus managed the shoot to avoid leaks and preserve intimacy. Every question was agreed with the Sussexes before entering the room, and any discussion of retakes happened offstage in a production area where Archewell’s executive producer, Ben Browning, watched on a monitor and gave approval. Reshoots, especially of emotional moments, were handled privately; crew members noticed some do-overs but were instructed that Meghan’s performances were spontaneous.

The Sussexes also provided Garbus with more than twenty hours of footage they had shot themselves. Among the raw clips was the morning after the Oprah interview, a grainy, unlit sequence in which Meghan announced, “Beyoncé’s just texted!” and the couple bantered lightly. Other moments looked staged: a call from Tyler Perry and a scene in which Harry held up a phone so Meghan could read a text from William, followed by an embrace and a suggested break. Crew members recalled Garbus leaving it to Meghan to hit the emotional beats without implying they were manufactured.

At the same time Harry was deep in work with ghostwriter J.R. Moehringer on an autobiography. Early drafts circulated among Random House editors and recorded intense accounts of Harry’s mental health struggles and bitter complaints about his family. Editors noted the drafts’ one-sided tone and Moehringer’s willingness to amplify Harry’s grievances. The ghostwriter, a self-declared republican who cared little for British history, sometimes added soap-opera flourishes to serve his client.

These attacks built up before and after the death of Prince Philip on April 9, 2021. Although Philip’s passing was expected, it did not slow Harry’s criticisms. He returned to St George’s Chapel for the funeral on April 17, worried it would trigger trauma and convinced Meghan would be unwelcome after the Oprah interview. The Queen, preparing for the service, was heard to say she was glad Meghan wasn’t coming. With COVID limits and the Queen masked, the funeral images generated global sympathy.

At the service Harry’s expression struck observers as flat while Charles looked anguished and William tense. Afterward, Kate persuaded Harry to walk with William back toward the castle, a moment televised and quickly read as a reconciliation. Harry, however, expected a genuine apology and accountability from Charles and William; in his view, reconciliation required them to beg forgiveness from him and Meghan.

Tensions boiled over in a meeting after the service near Frogmore. Harry later framed the meeting as his attempt to “find a way out” of months of conflict, claiming an atmosphere of threats and even snipers outside the chapel. After accusing his father and brother of colluding with the press, he told them, “You helped them against us.” William replied bluntly, “You’re delusional, Harry,” and Charles added that the Palace could not simply order the media to change its behavior.

Harry’s account to Moehringer portrayed Charles as indifferent and bewildered and cast the family as a kind of death cult he must escape. Editors reading those drafts felt Moehringer allowed Harry’s anger to go unchecked, turning private grievances into theatrical denunciations. Charles and William grew convinced that further communication was pointless: Harry spoke in language shaped by therapy and Meghan’s influence that his family did not understand.

On May 21, 2021, Apple TV released The Me You Can’t See, a series Harry co-produced with Oprah Winfrey about mental health. The show drew heavily on his personal torment and presented mental health as a primary lens for judging the family’s failures. Harry argued that the Royal Family’s silence and inaction had amounted to betrayal, linked Charles’s and others’ struggles to his own, and used therapy narratives to justify cutting ties. He compared his life to The Truman Show and described feeling trapped in the institution.

Harry also credited therapeutic experiences and ayahuasca for stabilizing him and giving him new insight. But his public accounts of when and why he entered therapy shifted over time. In 2017 he had publicly said William urged him to seek help after his breakdown; later versions shifted that initiative to Meghan in 2016 and suggested the family had provided no support after his earlier crises. Harry’s stories about whether he had told his family about Meghan’s suicidal thoughts also varied across tellings.

Meghan’s own accounts contained inconsistencies too. She said she had sought help twice and had been rebuffed by Buckingham Palace HR and by a psychoanalyst referral, but she did not say she had taken those complaints to senior family members. The differing versions from Harry and Meghan left factual gaps and invited outside skepticism. Piers Morgan’s line — “I wouldn’t believe Meghan Markle if she gave me a weather report” — became a touchstone for critics who portrayed Meghan’s disclosures as a performance to prompt relocation to California.

The Apple series was a ratings success and won sympathy from many viewers, especially men reluctant to talk about mental health. Critics, however, saw problems in a wealthy royal casting his family as villains while seeking public pity. The program amplified Harry’s versions and fed public debate; it damaged trust within the family and signaled to people close to the Sussexes that their campaign of accusation and narrative control had consequences beyond the show. Meghan perceived those consequences and sensed the beginning of a new problem even as Harry’s public standing remained largely intact.

Meghan knew the Sussex brand mattered in America, where people dislike perceived grifters. Her public image — living a celebrity life in Montecito while claiming royal privacy — risked appearing elitist and out of touch with the American Dream. Her team decided she needed a clear unique selling point to answer critics who called her famous but not relevant, so her publicists urged a rewrite of her backstory to make her more relatable.

Originally Meghan had described herself as someone who embraced farm-to-table food. Under advice, she altered that narrative to a tougher-sounding childhood, portraying herself as a latch-key kid surviving on cheap TV dinners. The change was meant to fit the familiar Hollywood arc of hardship and rise, replacing a detail that sounded privileged with one that aimed to generate sympathy.

While Meghan was heavily pregnant with their second child, the couple decided to use the Queen’s private nickname, Lilibet, for the unborn daughter and even registered lilibetdiana.com before the birth. The girl was born at Santa Barbara’s Cottage Hospital on June 4, 2021, attended by an all-female medical team. Harry phoned the Queen and later said she had approved the name, but the Palace briefed the BBC that the Queen had never been asked for permission, saying Harry had informed her rather than sought approval.

The BBC report prompted the Sussexes to threaten legal action. Their spokeswoman and lawyers insisted the Queen had been supportive; their law firm, Schillings, warned the BBC to apologize or face a defamation suit. When Buckingham Palace publicly backed the BBC and officials said the Queen was furious, the Sussexes withdrew the threat and their legal threat evaporated. The episode damaged the Sussexes’ claim of an unquestioned royal connection and angered Harry.

Security and protection became another battleground. On a return visit in late June and early July 2021, Harry found no armed police protection waiting at Heathrow. After a WellChild event at Kew Gardens, paparazzi surrounded their car and Harry told Edward Young he felt exposed to possible violent attack, citing his military past and fears of lone actors motivated by hatred or racism.

Young passed the complaint to Scotland Yard, which judged the situation undesirable but found no evidence of a specific assassination threat. Police said only a credible threat would justify permanent armed protection. Harry had earlier been warned that an independent government committee, Ravec, not the Palace, would decide his security. Ravec’s chair, Richard Mottram, weighed the risks and cost, and officials suspected the Sussexes intended to live abroad in California.

Whitehall officials, including Cabinet Secretary Mark Sedwill, told Harry that taxpayer-funded protection was for working royals and could not be provided to a non-working royal living overseas; Harry would have to pay privately. Mottram also refused to ask the formal Risk Management Board to review the case, saying the Sussexes’ new non-working status required a tailored approach. Harry felt excluded from decisions and accused Edward Young and Sedwill of obstructing and diminishing him.

On February 28, 2020 Ravec formally withdrew the Sussexes’ permanent police protection. Mottram explained the state could not accept a precedent of allowing individuals to pay for officers willing to risk their lives. Harry reacted with fury, seeing the withdrawal as punishment aimed at forcing them back under royal control, and began to mount a legal challenge.

Lawyers capitalized on Harry’s anger. Schillings and other counsel were already pursuing cases against tabloid publishers and encouraged Harry to seek judicial review of Mottram’s decision. In September 2021 Harry filed a High Court claim against the Home Office, arguing Ravec had treated him unlawfully and failed to explain why he had been treated less favorably and denied 24-hour armed guards.

The legal action embarrassed palace officials. Edward Young worried about a royal suing his own government in public, and the couple sought to keep parts of the proceedings private. Meanwhile the Sussexes toured the U.S. in September 2021 with strong local security, high-profile appearances, and careful messaging that emphasized their royal status and philanthropic work, including visits to the Twin Towers site, the United Nations, and the WHO.

Public relations were tightly controlled. For a school reading in Harlem, aides redecorated the classroom, adjusted lighting, and insisted on non-disclosure agreements to prevent negative comments. Journalists barred from the event later mocked Meghan’s expensive outfit and questioned the relevance of her book to the children, but the couple’s U.S. engagements boosted their American profile while their reputation in Britain weakened.

Trouble at home grew more personal when Jason Knauf said Meghan had not told the truth about a private letter and that he had been consulted and had given a copy to Omid Scobie. Schillings informed Meghan, and the couple filmed a staged response blaming William — footage later used in their Netflix documentary. Facing an appeal by the Mail on Sunday, Meghan, following legal advice, admitted in court that she had signed an inaccurate witness statement and apologized. The judges accepted her apology, calling it an unfortunate lapse of memory, and ruled in her favor.

Flush from legal and media successes, the Sussexes felt vindicated and powerful. The chapter closes by noting how their peak of influence and protection in America contrasted with growing isolation and controversy in Britain, and it hints that those at the top often fail to foresee the start of a decline.

An editor at Penguin Random House later described the early 2022 editing as chaotic. Moehringer sent multiple drafts of chapters that were repeatedly rewritten by several editors at different seniority levels, while Moehringer himself revised his drafts after comments from Harry and Harry’s wife. Editors found themselves working from several versions of the same chapters, which created confusion and redundant work.

A key source of tension was disagreement about the book’s core theme and how to portray Harry. Moehringer, encouraged to understand the Prince by interviewing friends in Britain, conducted many interviews that largely did not appear in the final text. Only a few acquaintances received brief mentions. Instead of developing a clear portrait of Harry’s life and context, Moehringer focused on vivid anecdotes and small grievances.

Moehringer magnified minor incidents—complaints about light in a basement, disputes over parking, sibling jealousy about bedrooms and weddings—rather than showing Harry as a wealthy, restless figure with complex motives. The editors worried that the manuscript lacked the broader context of Harry’s life and privileges and instead repeatedly emphasized similar grievances and family tensions.

The strongest material, editors agreed, was Harry’s dramatic accounts of fights with his father and brother. Descriptions of shouting matches and physical confrontations, and Harry’s portrayals of Charles as emotionally damaged and indecisive, brought energy to the manuscript and were repeatedly retained. Harry’s account that Charles feared being overshadowed by Meghan became a key charge in the narrative.

But many items in Harry’s recollections were inaccurate or uncorroborated, and Moehringer often did not check them. Quotes and scenes were presented as fact despite contradictions: the famous line attributed to Charles after Harry’s birth, different accounts of Diana’s reactions, and the timing and setting of the Queen Mother’s death were examples editors found problematic. The art exam story and the Palace’s denials were mishandled, with journalists’ and institutions’ official responses overlooked.

Remote interviews over Zoom slowed fact-checking, and even after in-person interviews in Montecito, Harry repeated claims that did not match public record. He told Moehringer that newspapers called him a coward for faking a knee injury; he linked press behavior to Caroline Flack’s suicide. Those specific assertions were false or unsupported by the available evidence and the coroner’s findings, respectively.

A central theme for Harry — that the press had racially targeted Meghan and driven the couple from Britain — shaped much of the book. Several concrete incidents Harry described, such as a Whole Foods visit where he said Meghan saw tabloids about her, proved false on inspection. Claims that major outlets had suppressed Meghan’s legal case against the Mail on Sunday also ran counter to the record that the case received wide coverage.

Tensions escalated in public when the Mail on Sunday planned to publish a story about Harry’s legal fight with the Home Office over police protection. Harry’s PR team and lawyers briefed that he had offered to pay for protection; later evidence and recollections by officials contradicted that claim. Harry sued the Mail for defamation, but the judge assigned to his case found the press lawyers had avoided saying he lied, while also noting the article could be seen as accusing him of intending to mislead and refusing to grant judgment without a trial.

Harry’s actions came amid wider troubles for the royal household, increasing scrutiny and embarrassment. Prince Andrew’s civil case and Charles’s association with a Saudi donor prompted headlines and internal crises. Reporting revealed both Charles’s charity connections and that Harry had once received a donation for his own charity, which he later cut off. These scandals intensified the public backdrop against which Harry’s book and legal disputes unfolded.

Inside Penguin Random House, fact-checkers were eventually hired to catch errors, but mistakes still slipped through, such as a fable about corgis and Queen Victoria. Harry’s dislike of certain Palace figures and his harsh judgments, for example of Paul Burrell, remained in the manuscript even when the background was more complicated. Friction between Harry, Moehringer and senior editors, including disagreements over photographs, deadlines and repetitive or raw denunciations of family members, became routine.

Editorially, the team accepted a subjective, voice-driven approach to the memoir to preserve Harry’s perspective. Moehringer argued that memory and feeling mattered as much as objective facts and invoked a Gonzo-like style that prioritized emotion and interpretation over verifiable detail. He added literary references and framed Harry’s grievances in a way that emphasized victimhood and heroism rather than strict factual accuracy.

Lawyers required some last-minute edits, removing certain names and moderating language, though critiques of specific media figures were allowed as fair comment. Editors also pressed to delete explicit allegations of racism by the royal household; Harry agreed to drop those passages. By June 2022 the manuscript had been cut from about 800 pages to roughly 400, with plans to translate and publish globally in the autumn after final touches following Harry’s return from London for the Queen’s jubilee.

Harry had not yet decided whether he would return to London for the jubilee with Meghan and his children. His absence would be seen as a serious insult and harm his image, yet he did not seem particularly troubled by that prospect. Edward Young and other palace aides grew suspicious after Harry’s conduct on a trip to Windsor in April 2022, and especially because the Sussexes were conducting an aggressive campaign in the media. The visit came as Harry was en route from Los Angeles to The Hague for the Invictus Games, one of his few clear achievements, launched in 2014 to help wounded servicemen recover through sport.

The Queen was deeply hurt by Harry’s move to California. As Lady Elizabeth Anson put it, “Turning one’s back on duty is completely alien to the Queen.” Anson recalled the Queen being upset with Harry even before his wedding, and their relationship suffered further after his Oprah interview. The family feared Harry’s media appearances were eroding the mystique of the Crown and creating fresh divisions.

Arriving in The Hague, Harry agreed to an NBC interview timed to eclipse the Queen’s ninety-sixth birthday and a new official photograph. Meghan also did a planned photocall wearing expensive jewelry, including a Cartier watch once owned by Diana. Harry told NBC they had a “really special relationship” and that he was “making sure that she’s protected and got the right people around her.” The interview drew sharp criticism in the British press for self-pity and hypocrisy.

Palace officials warned Harry to keep a low profile for the jubilee. He would not meet his brother, could not wear a uniform, and would sit apart from the senior royals at St Paul’s. During Trooping the Colour he would watch from a window with minor royals; he might be invited to lunch at Buckingham Palace but would not appear on the balcony if he attended.

On the first day, Harry and Meghan attended in a manner that felt distant and frostily received. The Queen, limited by mobility issues, later appeared on the balcony with William, Kate, and their children, projecting a secure line of succession. The Sussexes spent much of the jubilee at Frogmore, and attempts to get photographs of Harry and baby Lilibet with the Queen in Windsor were refused “at Her Majesty’s request.” Several family members left the room and the chill continued until the Sussexes returned to California.

Reconciliation did not happen. Insiders said there would be “no way back” if Harry’s book came out in the Queen’s lifetime or if Netflix used jubilee footage. The Archbishop of Canterbury visited and concluded both sides were “talking past each other.” Back in the U.S., Harry spoke to his biographer and agreed to remove some of the most poisonous exchanges with his father and brother. Despite irritation at Random House, the book remained scheduled for publication on the eve of Christmas.

Back in Montecito, the Sussexes watched Liz Garbus’s edit of the Netflix series, retitled Harry & Meghan, due for release in December 2022. Meghan had expected a portrait of her as a courageous reformer; Garbus instead framed her as the leader of identity politics and cast the couple as an innocent pair traduced by white monarchists. The film emphasised race and the Royal Family’s alleged prejudice and presented Meghan as a champion of human rights, feminism, and anti-racism while showing Harry as a guilt-ridden, complicit figure.

Garbus reinforced the Sussex narrative by bringing in commentators such as Kehinde Andrews, David Olusoga, and Afua Hirsch. Their contributions painted the monarchy and the Commonwealth as instruments of racial privilege and empire. Critics noted factual distortions in some of their work and pointed out omissions: the series ignored the Queen’s long public commitment to the Commonwealth and the complex realities of post‑colonial history, and it overstated links between the monarchy and ongoing white supremacy.

Andrews, in particular, argued that the Queen symbolised white supremacy and that little had changed for many people in former colonies. He and others suggested that Meghan’s grievances could be read primarily in racial terms, claiming that media coverage used the ‘‘angry black woman’’ trope. The film and its experts, however, did not produce the clear evidence critics expected to support those sweeping claims, nor did they address reporting about accusations that Meghan had bullied staff.

Selective editing extended beyond theory to image. Garbus omitted context that might have softened the film’s line, such as the Cambridges’ awkward but corrected meeting with children during a Jamaica visit. Netflix highlighted moments that reinforced Harry’s critique of his family, even including an embarrassing exchange in which Jamaica’s prime minister told William the country planned to become a republic.

A high-profile sequence showed Meghan recalling her first meeting with the Queen and exaggerating the curtsy as if it were a medieval farce. Harry allowed the scene to stay. The series also included private video clips of Meghan at emotional moments; their provenance and authenticity were unclear. Other scenes revealed contradictions and self-invention, such as a narrated car escape from paparazzi that Meghan later admitted had not occurred.

Meghan’s desire to be compared to Diana surfaced repeatedly. Harry drew parallels between Meghan and his mother, and he treated Diana’s presence as a guiding sign. Observers rejected the comparison: Diana’s youth, social isolation at court, public charisma, and tragic vulnerability differed from Meghan’s adult years of career and agency. Critics began calling Meghan an imposter rather than a successor to Diana’s role.

Separating her brand from Harry, Meghan prepared to appear alone in the public eye. Her Spotify podcast Archetypes was launched as a twelve‑part series to examine labels that limit women. The series was intended to cement her image as a thoughtful feminist leader, but production faltered. Staff reported Meghan frequently watered down ideas, lacked interviewing skill, and focused conversation back on herself. In her first interview with Serena Williams, Meghan talked mainly about her own experiences rather than drawing insight from Williams, undercutting the show’s promise.

Internal production tensions echoed earlier complaints from palace aides. Employees described difficult interactions and emotional strain; some left for therapy or took breaks. Non‑disclosure agreements limited public discussion, but reports suggested a pattern of staff feeling mistreated when projects did not go Meghan’s way. At the same time Meghan cultivated a maternal and relatable persona, critics accused her of having celebrity entitlement without the skills to match it.

To manage her image, Meghan agreed to press profiles. An interviewer for The Cut, Allison Davis, published a critical portrait despite Meghan’s expectations of solidarity. Meghan also made forceful claims about the tabloids and race, saying her children were targeted with racial slurs, and described herself as newly seen ‘‘as a black woman’’ after meeting Harry. She warned she could speak freely and hinted at future revelations, saying she had kept notes and a diary during her time in London.

Public reaction in the United States cooled as doubts grew about Meghan’s offerings. Celebrity endorsements and political overtures did not translate into consistent support: some entertainers mocked her, and Kamala Harris’s campaign declined her help. Harry sought to regain moral authority on global stages, speaking at United Nations events and linking his family story to wider crises. But his speeches drew critiques for overreaching comparisons, and reports of impractical ideas, such as imagined interviews with world leaders, undermined his credibility. The chapter closes with the Sussexes briefly returning to Britain for events and then flying back to California, still largely outside the royal loop and unaware of some unfolding family turmoil.

Early on 8 September 2022, Charles was at Dumfries House in Scotland preparing for a TV interview when he was told his mother was seriously ill. He flew by helicopter to Balmoral, checked on the Queen and, reassured she was stable, returned to Birkhall. He called William and Harry and told them to fly to Scotland; William left just after 2 p.m., while Harry was delayed, arguing on the phone at Frogmore. Charles later learned the Queen was dying while walking in the woods and was told she had died at 3:10 p.m., ending a seventy-year reign.

At Frogmore, Harry and Meghan hesitated about travel. Meghan contacted the PR firm Sunshine Sachs in Los Angeles to alert the media that the Sussexes were coming. When Charles heard Meghan planned to attend, he rang Harry and objected, saying Meghan was unwelcome because she had attacked the monarchy and had refused the Queen’s invitations. Charles told Harry, "I will not tolerate it!" Harry answered, "Don't even think of talking like that about my wife." William, unwilling to wait, had already ordered the plane to depart.

Harry later claimed the plane’s departure was "a deliberate snub"; William’s staff said Harry had not contacted them. William landed in Aberdeen at 3:50 p.m.; Harry flew north later and grew angrier as he thought about William becoming Prince of Wales and the Duchy of Cornwall income. The Queen’s death was announced at 6:30 p.m. Harry arrived at Balmoral to learn Charles, Camilla and William had gone to Birkhall for dinner and to sleep. Princess Anne took Harry to see the Queen’s body; he returned to London the next morning on a commercial flight and later told CBS, "I was not invited."

The state funeral was set for 19 September. The Sussexes waited ten days at Frogmore. On the third day after the death, William and Kate decided to meet mourners in Windsor and, at the last moment, invited Harry and Meghan so the family would not appear to exclude them. Meghan took time for hair and makeup. The two couples drove together in silence to the castle gates and then split to greet people. The crowd focused on Harry and Meghan; their public interaction looked warm to some observers but the couple left feeling coldly treated. Harry complained the family showed "absolutely no willingness to reconcile," and Kate reportedly found the walkabout extremely hard.

Funeral planning raised specific disputes about Harry’s role and dress. After talks with palace officials, Harry agreed not to wear military uniform during the procession pulling the Queen’s coffin, but was later allowed to wear a uniform while standing vigil in Westminster Abbey. When his uniform arrived, the Queen’s cipher EIIR had been removed from the shoulder, because he was no longer a working royal. He protested when he saw that Prince Andrew’s uniform retained the insignia, and officials explained differences in what had been returned.

Charles resisted stripping Harry and Meghan of their titles but did disinvite them from a Buckingham Palace reception for heads of state.
