He delayed signing letters patent that would restore HRH status to Archie and Lilibet and planned to amend the Regency Act to remove Harry as a counsellor of state, a role tied to having a British home.

While Harry focused on his relationship with the family, Meghan reorganized their communications team. Sunshine Sachs staffers resigned and Christine Schirmer was appointed to lead media for Archewell. Variety agreed to delay publishing Meghan’s interview until after the funeral; in it she paid late tribute to the Queen as "a shining example of female leadership." Back in Montecito, Meghan framed herself as unheard and quoted a Greek poet: "What didn’t you do to bury me? But you forgot that I was a seed."

On 19 September, Harry entered Westminster Abbey with visible indignation. In his brief tribute he called the Queen his "guiding compass" and quoted her line, "Life, of course, consists of final partings as well as first meetings." Meghan sat with Sophie, Countess of Wessex; tensions were visible. Camera angles and seating kept Harry and William separated. Meghan publicly attributed the cooling to racism and presented herself as a victim; reporters described Harry as tormented and grievance-marked.

After two weeks in Britain, the Sussexes left for Los Angeles on a commercial flight. A request to travel on Air Force One was turned down by the White House to avoid offending the Royal Family, and Jill Biden had declined an invitation to the Invictus Games that Harry had extended. The Sussexes released their own official photo after Buckingham Palace published the new royal family portrait, a move seen as a calculated response and part of their ongoing public positioning.

Harry remained determined to press on with his memoir and media projects. His book Spare had been prepared while the Queen was alive, with multiple language editions and audio ready for production. Despite the timing, he showed little public concern for its effect on the Queen's reputation, and promotional material described the book as offering "raw, unflinching honesty" and "hard-won wisdom about the eternal power of love over grief."

While the Queen lay in state, Prince Harry approved the details of a legal claim against Associated Newspapers, owner of the Mail group. His lawyer David Sherborne had spent months recruiting high-profile claimants, the most notable being Baroness Doreen Lawrence, whose son Stephen was murdered in 1993 and whose case exposed police corruption after a bold Daily Mail front page in 1997. That coverage helped break the stalemate, leading to official inquiry and later convictions; Lawrence had appeared grateful to the paper before being invited into the new action against it.

In January 2022 Harry messaged Lawrence that there was information she would want to know about and Sherborne soon met her. He presented a statement allegedly signed by a private investigator, Gavin Burrows, claiming he had been paid by the Mail to intercept voicemails, collect phone bills and obtain medical records. Burrows’s confession named other targets, including Harry, Hugh Grant and Doreen Lawrence, and Sherborne also showed Lawrence a statement signed by Harry endorsing Burrows’s claims. Lawrence said she felt numb with anger and trauma and agreed to join the claim alongside Harry and other celebrities such as Elton John and Elizabeth Hurley.

Associated Newspapers responded angrily, calling the allegations groundless and noting that Burrows had links to convicted hackers. Then Associated produced a second statement, signed by the same Gavin Burrows, denying the version Sherborne had shown. That contradiction raised questions about the authenticity of evidence central to the claim and made Harry’s case much harder to prove without a clear money trail.

Harry’s legal team appointed independent lawyers to search Associated’s archives for payments to investigators, but finding direct payments was difficult and costly. The projected trial cost reached £38 million. Some of Harry’s messages to Lawrence went missing, yet Sherborne continued to assert links between Mail stories and unlawful investigator activity, claiming the group had paid about £3 million to private investigators. He pointed to Mail on Sunday articles bearing intimate details of Harry’s private life and alleged they were supplied by bribes and illegal methods. The Mail denied this, saying reporters cultivated sources and used lawful reporting techniques.

Observers criticized Harry for being manipulated by lawyers who earned large fees from privacy claimants, a point that surfaced in a complaint to the press regulator IPSO. Harry also lost a separate complaint about Instagram photos showing him near untethered elephants; the photos had been cropped and his complaint was rejected. Still committed to combating press intrusion, he and his lawyers prepared claims against other papers, reading decades of coverage and treating many hostile reports as illegally sourced. Harry believed the media’s hostility justified both his forthcoming book and a Netflix series.

Netflix moved quickly, seeing the Sussexes as attention magnets and planning marketing tied to seasons of royal visibility. The company timed promotional material to coincide with William and Kate’s Earthshot Prize visit to Boston, which the Sussexes countered with a New York appearance. The palace suffered an unexpected distraction when an 83-year-old former lady-in-waiting, Lady Susan Hussey, spoke with Ngozi Fulani at a Buckingham Palace reception. Fulani later tweeted that Hussey had asked, Where do you really come from? The tweet went viral before facts were checked, Hussey was dismissed within hours, and Buckingham Palace panicked at the potential confirmation of the Sussexes’ charge of institutional racism.

Netflix’s trailer accused the Royal Family and parts of the British press of a campaign to destroy Meghan, using dramatic music and selective editing. Some footage was misleading or taken out of context, though a genuine clip showed Harry shepherding Meghan away from flashbulbs in 2018. The Sussexes said they hoped the series would help others heal; Meghan later told Variety the series was not how they would have told their story.

The Sussexes also received the Robert F. Kennedy Ripple Award in New York, presented by Kerry Kennedy two days after the Earthshot events and just before the Netflix premiere. The award recognized them for challenging the royal power structure. Meghan used the gala to speak about racism and to reveal that she had contemplated suicide, asking listeners not to let others feel alone. Their arrival in designer clothes and Diana’s aquamarine ring drew heavy media attention. Some Americans praised them, but others called the award and the ceremony absurd or misplaced.

When the Netflix series aired, it broke viewing records with millions watching in Britain and tens of millions worldwide. Reactions were mixed and often hostile in Britain: critics called the project self-promotional and repetitive, and polls showed low public support for the Sussexes compared with William and Kate. Still, a significant minority believed the royal family was racist and many thought the Sussexes should be invited to the coronation. Buckingham Palace judged the series less damaging than feared and said the couple had overplayed their hand.

Constitutional realities made Harry’s presence at the coronation desirable for the monarchy, but the Palace set conditions: no uniform, no medals, and no official role. Harry refused to commit without a formal apology or acceptance of responsibility from the family and asked for a meeting to try to resolve outstanding issues, placing the ball in the Palace’s court. He continued to prepare for more interviews and the publication of his memoir Spare. For Christmas, the Sussexes spent a quiet holiday in Montecito with family, while King Charles hosted a large Sandringham gathering and issued a televised message praising togetherness and the work of William and Kate, making no mention of Harry.

The Spare publication party in Santa Barbara was low key and poorly attended by Random House staff, even as the book sold 1.43 million copies on day one and 3.2 million in its first week. Editors had expected a big seller but not the fastest-selling non-fiction book in history. Everyone acknowledged the book’s pull: a celebrity memoir full of intimate revelations, even if it was not the project some editors had hoped for. Harry recorded his own fifteen-hour, thirty-seven-minute audio version, so listeners heard his attacks on his family in his voice.

Meghan was notably restrained at the release. Months earlier she had stepped back from Harry’s promotion while trying to revive her commercial brand. Advised to shed the image of a sour duchess, she planned a California-style entrepreneurial relaunch: reviving her blog The Tig, developing scripted romcoms and documentaries for Netflix, and hiring Tracy Ryerson and Nishika Kumble to run scripted content. Two senior staff—Mandana Dayani and Rebecca Sananes—left Archewell, and the foundation paid £90,000 to Katie McCormick Lelyveld’s PR company to help manage publicity.

Harry’s publicists steered him to a moral framing: the book was meant to reform the monarchy for the sake of his brother’s children. He made this claim repeatedly in TV interviews, even while publicizing intimate and crude personal episodes from the book. Anticipating attacks on his credibility, ghostwriter J. R. Moehringer had woven into the text defenses about memory and memoir, quoting the idea that the line between memory and fact is blurry. That approach invited criticism that the book blurred interpretation with objective fact.

A promotional video that linked Harry’s work to Nelson Mandela provoked anger from Mandela’s family. During publication week, TV interviews were filmed at the San Ysidro Ranch. ITV’s Tom Bradby, who would ask tough questions, found a sincere and combative Harry. Bradby confronted him about the Sussexes’ Oprah allegation that the family had raised concerns about Archie’s skin color; Harry denied he or Meghan had accused his relatives of racism, instead blaming press coverage and citing only unconscious bias. Viewers who remembered Meghan’s Oprah interview found the exchange contradictory.

Harry attacked the British press and accused family members of feeding stories to tabloids. He said William and Buckingham Palace had deliberately trashed the Sussexes’ reputations and that the family showed no willingness to reconcile. He insisted he had written the book to protect himself and wanted his family to apologize, hoping for private reconciliation while continuing to air public criticisms. Asked what he would say to William, he replied that it would be said in private.

Camilla became another target. Harry accused her of leaking details of her first meeting with William and portrayed her as a sinister presence in his parents’ marriage. Some of that material had been removed from the manuscript, but he repeated attacks on camera. He later softened some comments, saying he had compassion for Camilla, but the public back-and-forth suggested a lack of restraint and ongoing family rupture.

A premature Spanish release and rapid translations gave British tabloids fuel for daily headlines. One of the most inflammatory passages was Harry’s description of killing twenty-five Taliban; senior military figures, including Colonel Tim Collins, condemned it as unlike British values and a betrayal of the armed forces. Harry and his team accused the press of twisting his words and argued that publication endangered his family, a line that became a recurring defense when criticism followed.

On publication day Harry was told by Buckingham Palace, on King Charles’s orders, that he had been expelled from Frogmore; the decision and repayment of renovation costs were handled quietly, and Harry soon listed Montecito as his primary residence. He toured U.S. television, telling Stephen Colbert he felt “incredibly free” and oscillating between crude anecdotes and claims of compassion. British papers were scathing, U.S. commentary was mixed, and polls showed sharp differences in public sympathy: British support for the Sussexes collapsed while American approval remained split, with greater support among nonwhite voters.

Backlash included criticism of Moehringer for privileging memoir over verifiable fact; some editors and publishers worried such defenses would not withstand fact-checking. Friends and acquaintances in Britain felt betrayed, writing angrily to Harry about his disclosures. Harry warned that deleted pages contained a great deal more dirt about family members he declined to make public; reconciliation talks faltered. Palace insiders said healing would require both sides to stop attacking, but emotions were raw and no neutral mediator emerged before the coronation plans proceeded.

Outside the royal story, Harry’s post-publication life included a high-profile conversation with Dr. Gabor Maté, launched as part of a marketed mental-health narrative and bundled with book sales. Maté’s politics and praise of psychedelics invited controversy; he later admitted media reaction hurt him. Harry also took paid roles as a mental-health coach with BetterUp and continued a routine of family life, exercise, cannabis use, and extensive legal fights in London. Through the frenzy Meghan remained publicly quiet, leaving Harry to bear the media and legal battles largely on his own.

Six weeks after Harry’s book tour, Meghan promoted Clevr Blends coffee in a video and owned a stake in the company. The powdered drinks claimed to help sleep and anxiety. She also endorsed latte powders, Tatcha skincare, and high-priced Eberjey bathrobes. Earlier influence over fashion was clear when a £545 Strathberry bag she carried sold out in 2017; years later a Cesta bag she used also caused a sudden spike in sales. By 2023 she had interests in more than five brands and described these investments as preparation for “investing in myself.”

Harry insisted their move to California was not about money, saying they were dedicated to service, but outsiders saw Meghan building a celebrity business. Her public charity work often doubled as brand promotion. She signed with Ari Emanuel’s William Morris Endeavor to develop her global profile and posed in a photo shoot as a Hollywood figure. What followed were several setbacks and shifts in public attention.

Once celebrated as Time’s Most Influential People, the Sussexes later found themselves excluded from some A-list events. They were not invited to Barack Obama’s sixtieth birthday or the Clooneys’ New York party, despite past connections. The change reflected a broader cooling in elite social circles, driven in part by the Royal Family’s displeasure with the couple.

Title disputes for their children became a major point of tension. Meghan had previously said titles caused “a lot of pain” and had suggested the family had once planned to deny Archie a title because of race. Yet by 2023 the couple pressed for royal ranks for their children. On March 3, 2023, they held a christening in Montecito for 21-month-old Lilibet, blessed by the Bishop of Los Angeles and attended by Diana’s sisters, with Tyler Perry named godfather. The event was announced in People magazine; the Palace’s court circular ignored it. After initial refusal, the King later permitted recognition of the grandchildren’s titles, and Meghan moved to change their surnames to Sussex.

Meghan focused on rebuilding her public image and earning on her own, and she stepped back from the campaign to fight tabloid abuse, though she supported Harry’s efforts for police protection. Harry believed he was vulnerable to threats and thought his father might have helped. Tensions with Palace officials grew when Harry’s calls to the King were often blocked and he felt rebuffed, which he took as part of a wider conspiracy. He then briefed People magazine, worsening relations.

Harry returned to London in late March 2023 for hearings in his claims against the Mail group, bringing two suits: a defamation claim and a newer claim alleging unlawful bugging. His lawyers relied on financial ledgers and records linked to the 2012 Leveson inquiry. Those files had been treated as confidential under Judge Leveson’s terms; Harry’s team said an investigative journalist provided the material. The records showed payments by the Mail group to private investigators, raising questions about whether unlawful methods had been used against Harry.

In Courtroom 76, Harry sat with supporters while the Mail sought to dismiss the case. His team planned to call a string of witnesses with criminal records or controversial reputations: private investigators who admitted arrests or convictions, a former “blagger” who denied illegal acts, and a former reporter accused of fabrication who had been paid large sums by claimants’ operatives. Contradictory statements and payments by some witnesses, and accusations that members of the claimants’ research team tried to evade limitation rules, created a complex and murky evidentiary picture. After a four-day hearing, Judge Matthew Nicklin described the explanation for how the Mail’s ledgers surfaced as “theoretical” and “fanciful,” and he barred the files from evidence; that decision was later overturned by the government in the public interest.

At the same time Harry negotiated his role at the coronation. He accepted limits: no procession, no balcony appearance for Meghan or the children, and a quick return to California after the service. Buckingham Palace announced the King was pleased Harry would attend but disappointed the family would not be present. Meghan publicly appealed for an end to the “exhausting circus,” then two weeks later reappeared with Harry at a Los Angeles Lakers event in a staged public moment that suggested separate career paths. Reports said the couple’s display was choreographed and emotionally strained.

Harry’s wider High Court action accused News UK and Mirror papers of unlawful information-gathering and accused the press and parts of the establishment of harming his mental health and attacking Meghan. He also alleged a secret understanding between senior Palace figures and News UK that had prevented action against the papers and that News UK had paid William compensation, which he said showed unequal treatment. The Palace and News UK denied any secret agreement, and Harry’s lawyers had no documentary proof. The claim also raised questions because he had not raised the alleged deal early in his pleadings.

In April, at a pretrial hearing, Judge Timothy Fancourt sharply criticized aspects of Harry’s case, calling the notion of a secret agreement “Alice in Wonderland” and finding parts of Harry’s account inconsistent, vague, and lacking credibility. Despite those criticisms, the judge allowed Harry’s lawyers access to certain emails from News UK and the Palace and permitted a claim to proceed over alleged unlawful investigations and invasions of privacy. The path to trial remained complicated by disputes over evidence, witness credibility, and procedural delays, with the Mirror trial still set to follow after the News UK proceedings.

Twenty-four hours before the crowning service, Harry arrived at Heathrow from Los Angeles and spent his last night at Frogmore under armed police protection. He had been denied invitations to pre-ceremony receptions and the three days of celebrations afterward, a visible sign of his isolation from the family and the institution he had once served. His presence in Britain felt limited and controlled.

After the jubilee and the Queen’s funeral, London again prepared for a major royal parade. Many Britons welcomed a reaffirmation of the monarchy, and Charles, now King, stepped forward to demand allegiance while promising service. Yet the institution faced real challenges: some Commonwealth countries were moving toward republics, younger and immigrant communities questioned a hereditary system, and scandals involving Prince Andrew and Harry had damaged public respect. Critics in the Establishment faulted Charles for not resolving those embarrassments decisively, and he worried about how his and Camilla’s popularity would affect the Crown’s future.

Public opinion mattered more than ever. Diana’s popularity had shown the monarchy’s dependence on communal support, and the family’s mishandling of her death had exposed vulnerability. Camilla remained unpopular because of her past affair with Charles and her earlier relationship with Andrew Parker Bowles, while Charles’s own standing had been strengthened by long-term causes such as environmental advocacy. Most of the monarchy’s practical weight now rested on William and Kate, whose popularity was seen as the secure linchpin for the succession and for a smaller, active royal family.

Meghan’s absence from the service provoked debate. Supporters said the Sussexes had been treated badly—late notice about the Queen’s death, refusal to wear uniform at the funeral, and a withdrawn palace reception invitation. Others suggested Meghan was angry about the King’s response to her claims of racism, or that she scorned the royal family’s traditions. No single public explanation satisfied everyone.

In the driving rain Harry arrived at the abbey with Prince Andrew, whose own relations with the King and the family were strained and who had recently behaved rudely toward a diplomat. Harry, wearing an unpressed morning suit and his medals, walked up the aisle alone, largely ignored his brother and sister-in-law, and sat in the third row beside Jack Brooksbank. He told Brooksbank, “I’m fed up with the way they treat me,” and watched the front row as Princess Anne’s plume momentarily blocked his view.

During the service, after the crown was placed on Charles, Harry intoned, “God Save King Charles. Long Live King Charles.” The heralds proclaimed “Vivat Regina Camilla,” a moment that underscored Charles’s success in elevating Camilla despite lingering public hostility. Harry did not bow to the new Queen, and the family’s section showed no public acknowledgment. William knelt, pledged loyalty, and kissed his father, displaying a close bond Harry had once sacrificed and now appeared to reject. The King, aware of how scandals had toppled other monarchies, saw Harry as a serious internal threat.

Thirty-five minutes after the service ended, Harry was back at Heathrow and left on a midafternoon flight to Los Angeles, having spent twenty-nine hours in Britain. At a palace reception later, Charles toasted his grandchildren and Archie “wherever he is,” while guests said William had decided that afternoon not to invite Harry to the palace event and that reconciliation seemed unlikely. That evening at Windsor, William publicly praised his father and, in a measured rebuke to the Sussexes, concluded with “God save the King.”

On 16 May 2023 Harry and Meghan returned to New York to relaunch their public profile. Meghan was to receive the Ms Foundation for Women’s World of Vision Award at the foundation’s fiftieth anniversary, honored for her global advocacy for women and girls. The ceremony promised publicity and fundraising that the couple hoped would restore their standing after recent bad press.

Their public appeal had slipped sharply in the United States: Meghan’s popularity had plunged by thirty-six points to about minus thirty-three, and Harry’s had fallen by forty-five points to around minus twenty-two. Critics and comedians mocked the couple’s confessional tone, and the New York Times asked whether their repeated focus on themselves had worn thin. Before the event, Meghan hosted a Montecito lunch with Hollywood friends including Cameron Diaz and Gwyneth Paltrow. Gloria Steinem praised Meghan, and Meghan called Steinem her mentor. Meghan also criticized her earlier work on Deal or No Deal as objectifying.

The Sussexes stayed in an Upper East Side private home rather than a hotel and hired TorchStone security. On the same day Harry was also fighting in a London court the Home Office’s refusal to provide armed protection. His lawyer said paparazzi had chased the couple before and argued that Harry feared for Meghan’s life; the hearing would continue the next day.

At the Ziegfeld Ballroom the couple entered by a back door to avoid posed photos, frustrating waiting photographers. Meghan gave a six-minute speech about feminist influences, recalling how her mother’s subscription to Ms magazine helped shape her. The organizers hoped to raise $100 million from the thousand guests, but the event raised less than $1.5 million.

As they left, a scramble between photographers and the Sussexes’ convoy became a major story. Harry’s team described a two-hour, near-catastrophic pursuit involving cars, bikes, and dangerous driving, and a security member said the car had reached eighty miles per hour. The NYPD and New York’s mayor said the scenes were chaotic but not a two-hour chase, with no collisions or injuries. BackGrid’s photographs showed Meghan smiling in the SUV and reported that Harry’s driver had made risky maneuvers. The couple briefly used a yellow cab, then returned to their SUV and went to a private residence.

Authorities investigated and reached different conclusions. The 19th Precinct chief said the Sussexes did not enter the station or file a complaint; a cab driver said he did not feel threatened. The Manhattan district attorney declined to bring charges, calling the incident unpleasant but not criminal and saying it was not a near-death experience. The NYPD’s intelligence chief later called the photographers’ conduct reckless but not indictable, while pledging enhanced security resources for future visits.

The chase dominated headlines and damaged the couple’s image. Celebrities who might host or support them worried that Meghan’s publicity could overshadow events. In response to bad publicity, Meghan sought to demonstrate service and empathy: on 24 May she flew to Uvalde, Texas, to lay flowers and meet grieving families after a school shooting. At the same time her estranged father, Thomas Markle, suffered a stroke and she did not visit him. The visit to Uvalde highlighted a tension in their narrative: claiming vulnerability in Britain while living publicly in a country where gun violence is a real danger.

On the legal front, Harry pressed for photos to be surrendered and called for prosecutions, confronting American free-press protections. In London, he lost that day’s claim for armed police protection and was ordered to pay a portion of the government’s legal costs, though a larger challenge to the Home Office and a case against the Mirror Group were still pending. The episode left the couple facing skepticism about their accounts and anxious to protect their narrative as they pursued further legal battles.

Harry arrived a day late to the London courtroom and did not apologize, telling the judge he had to celebrate Lilibet’s second birthday in Montecito. On 6 June 2023 he became the first member of the Royal Family since 1891 to give witness testimony in a civil trial, facing Mirror Group Newspapers after years of litigation and millions spent on preparation. Dozens of boxes of documents surrounded the lawyers as David Sherborne led the case that the Mirror had not offered enough compensation to stop the trial.

In the witness box Harry asked to be called Prince Harry, though the judge ruled he should be addressed as Sir. Mirror Group had already paid about £105 million to roughly six hundred claimants for illegal information gathering. Harry alleged the Mirror spent £10.7 million on 114 unlawful investigations and made numerous payments that targeted his phone and the phones of friends and staff, including Guy Pelly, Mark Dyer and his former nanny Tiggy Legge-Bourke. He described the Mirror journalists’ behaviour as utterly vile.

His legal team uncovered evidence by tracing private investigators’ call records and the Mirror’s payments. They found signs that friends had been targeted, though some records had been destroyed. Sherborne called it a flood of illegality over decades and pointed to Piers Morgan’s tenure at the paper. Morgan denied authorizing hacking. Omid Scobie’s evidence about Morgan was accepted by the judge, though Harry downplayed his own relationship with Scobie.

In a 55-page witness statement Harry presented himself as repeatedly pursued and damaged by the press. He described being swarmed by paparazzi, humiliated by intrusive coverage, and pushed toward excess by tabloids that enjoyed knocking him down. He said the coverage caused depression, paranoia and a loss of trust in those around him.

Harry gave many examples tied to his relationship with Chelsy Davy, whom he met as a teenager and dated on and off until 2011. He said Mirror stories relied on voicemail material and other private information: reports of her arrivals at Heathrow, a secret holiday in Bazaruto, a surprise meeting in Argentina, repeated stories of arguments and phone calls, and the Sunday Mirror headlines announcing their breakup and later reporting she had a new partner. Those articles, he said, were deeply hurtful and seemed only possible if phones had been hacked.

He also alleged the Mirror had intruded into private calls involving his father and that the paper had accessed messages connected to his mother. His lawyers found payments for hacking Diana’s phone messages, which made him physically sick to consider. The earlier convictions in the phone-hacking scandal, such as those of Clive Goodman and Neville Thurlbeck in 2006, confirmed that colleagues had engaged in illegal practices, though Mirror journalists continued to rely on unlawfully obtained material.

Harry felt officials and institutions had turned a blind eye, and he expected deference in court. He was unprepared for aggressive cross-examination by the Mirror’s lawyer, Andrew Green, who showed that most of the stories shown to Harry could be traced to ordinary reporting methods: quotes from other newspapers, BBC reports, palace statements, interviews, eyewitnesses or people like Mark Dyer. Green argued there was no evidence that those articles came from hacking Harry’s own phone.

Under pressure, Harry often answered that he did not know, had not read articles at the time, or could not remember. He admitted some stories could not have come from hacking, such as a description of his twelfth birthday before he owned a phone. When Green pressed him on whether some headlines reflected normal republishing, Harry conceded similarities but could not explain them. The press reported his testimony as weak and speculative after the first day.

On the second day his answers were short and he struggled with the idea of public interest in stories about him. He took many critical reports as personal attacks rather than legitimate journalism. A grammar point about a headline left him embarrassed when he did not recognize the term Hooray Henry. Scotland Yard’s investigation, Green said, found no direct evidence that Harry’s phone had been hacked, and even convicted hackers did not testify that his phone was targeted. Harry ultimately admitted there was no direct proof of his own phone being hacked but maintained that losing would be an injustice.

Public reaction called the case total speculation, which Harry found destructive. Sherborne framed the lawsuit as part of a campaign to reform the press, and he understood Harry’s legal advantages: prior findings that Mirror hacking was widespread and the reluctance of courts to reject royal claims. Judge Fancourt later spent six months considering evidence that friends’ phones had been hacked, and when he prepared his judgment the balance of factors suggested the Mirror faced a likely defeat. For Meghan, the publicity around Harry’s trial underlined the need to avoid further controversy.

The press turned savage. Rolling Stone declared the couple in a “flop era,” and Hollywood commentators mocked Harry as an overprivileged figure who had lost his edge. Publicists even denied small stories about where Harry occasionally slept as the headlines piled up. The coverage made clear that the Sussexes’ brand was under siege and that public opinion had shifted fast.

Meghan faced hard realities about what she could achieve. She recognized that casting the Sussexes as a global humanitarian power couple would not pay the bills. She admired entrepreneurial lifestyle stars—Gwyneth Paltrow, Martha Stewart—and icons like Michelle Obama and Oprah Winfrey, whose advice she had prized: that success comes from turning “the essence of who you are” into a product that helps people feel seen and enough. That idea shaped her ambition to become a commercial, lifestyle celebrity.

Her marriage to Harry both propelled and complicated those plans. Free from the Palace, she wanted to sell endorsements in food, wellness, and fashion and to build an Instagram-style platform like the Kardashians. She pushed her agent, Ari Emanuel, to reshape her public image as an independent celebrity. Emanuel and other agents secured a Netflix lifestyle series and other opportunities as Meghan described herself as still in an “incubation” or “ideation” stage of developing a brand.

Everything turned worse in mid-June when headlines said Spotify had “ditched” the Sussexes. Anonymous sources accused them of missing productivity targets after delivering one season of Archetypes in two and a half years. Industry figures were blunt: Jeremy Zimmer said Meghan was not a great audio talent, and others said fame alone did not equal skill. Harry had also publicly condemned Spotify for Joe Rogan’s Covid misinformation, a comment that complicated their relationship with the platform.

Netflix and other allies tried to blunt the damage. Reed Hastings praised their instincts even as Netflix had already rejected Meghan’s Pearl project. Netflix pushed to secure nominations and hyped a reported $3 million deal for film rights to Meet Me at the Lake, with the Sussexes attached to produce. But the loudest blow came from Bill Simmons, who called them “grifters,” mocked Harry’s podcast attempts, and publicly ridiculed their media ambitions. The public fallout inside their Montecito home was intense; witnesses said Harry “fell to pieces” and Meghan was distraught.

Meghan publicly processed the rejection on a podcast, asking how to respond to a “crushing no” and urging resilience—digging deep and getting back up. The Spotify termination was an embarrassment and a financial loss, reportedly around $20 million, but Netflix remained committed to multiple projects and advances estimated collectively at about $100 million. Emanuel told the press offers were still coming and hinted at major deals, while rumors resurfaced about high-fashion partnerships. Critics, however, argued the missing ingredient was originality rather than publicity.

The Sussexes reorganized their staff, promoting local contacts into key roles, but bad publicity continued to shadow even private appearances. Meghan hoped her forty-second birthday in August would reset the story; publicists leaked a dinner at a Montecito restaurant but noticed a striking silence: no congratulations from the Royal Family and no messages from several A-list friends. Harry appeared glum at a Beyoncé event while Meghan socialized separately with a circle of loyal women friends and wellness entrepreneurs. She skipped a meeting with the Beckhams as Harry attended an Inter Miami match with celebrities, and four days after her birthday Harry retreated from the public eye to regroup.

Harry arrived in Japan with Ignacio "Nacho" Figueras, a friend and fellow Los Padres polo player, and at first felt at ease. The trip was paid for by the International Sports Promotion Society, founded by Haruhisa Handa, which had given millions to Sentebale and pledged more money while sponsoring polo events in the US and Singapore. Harry agreed to speak at ISPS events in Tokyo and then play in Singapore, telling the audience that charity had always been his life.

The Tokyo engagements went badly for Harry. He had expected a small dinner but Midori Miyazaki, ISPS's executive director, had arranged large arena and gala appearances, which left him feeling manipulated. Worse, Harry learned that many people at the events had never heard of Sentebale, and he became frustrated when he discovered more serious problems waiting at home.

Sentebale began in 2006 when Harry and Prince Seeiso founded the charity to help children affected by HIV in Lesotho and Botswana. The chapter recounts early mismanagement: in 2008 only a small part of funds raised was spent on orphans while directors enjoyed high pay and perks. A new CEO had improved matters, but by 2022 local staff complained about pay and conditions, some faced disciplinary action, and donor-funded projects were abandoned. Harry had not ordered the CEO to visit Lesotho since 2019, and locals assumed Harry was disengaged.

Sophie Chandauka, newly appointed chairwoman, found the charity financially fragile. Annual spending outpaced reliable income, the small London office was costly, and the famous polo events generated limited net profit. Many fundraising methods relied on Harry's profile and personal contacts rather than institutional donors. Chandauka warned that modern corporate donors required strong governance and transparent accounts, not informal fundraising by celebrities.

In private meetings in Tokyo and on the flight to Singapore, Chandauka delivered hard branding news: a survey of fifty organizations showed Harry's personal brand had fallen, and Meghan’s presence damaged his appeal in some markets, especially the US. Corporate donors had grown wary after controversial engagements and online criticism. Harry was surprised and defensive, comparing himself to public figures who had survived scandals, but the financial consequences were clear: sponsors and polo donors had withdrawn support.

Harry’s awkward behavior at the Singapore match and his refusal to fully cooperate with Chandauka strained their relationship. After Miyazaki told them Handa might end sponsorship, Harry blamed Chandauka for the fallout and refused to accept that Spare and the Netflix series had damaged his brand value. He insisted Sentebale relied too much on him and demanded higher profits from future polo matches. Chandauka came to realize Harry expected to retain control while asking her to fix governance and fundraising problems.

At a crucial board meeting Harry agreed to major changes. The London office would close, management would move to Lesotho, and the charity would broaden its mission from an HIV focus to general children’s health and education under "Project Pivot." He also committed to visit Lesotho in late 2024 for the first time in five years. The trustees and marketing expert Jonathan Mildenhall told him bluntly that his image had suffered and that fundraising in America would require a different pitch, avoiding a victim narrative.

Tensions with Miyazaki came to a head in New York when Chandauka met her to try to restore goodwill. Miyazaki was furious at Harry’s objections to the Tokyo over-exposure and, when Chandauka asked Handa to double his gift, Miyazaki withdrew support. Her departure cost Sentebale about 23 percent of its income and provoked a bitter exchange on Zoom in which Harry accused Chandauka of harming his and Meghan’s reputations with the brand audit.

Meanwhile another charity Harry supported, African Parks, faced serious accusations. Survival International alleged that the charity's armed rangers persecuted indigenous Baka people, expelled them from their lands, and committed violence while working to protect parks and combat insurgents. Harry had become president in 2017, then stepped down as president to join the governing board after an independent review was commissioned. The episode raised questions about his judgment and his ties to wealthy donors.

Outside his charities, Harry faced legal and reputational pressures. The conservative Heritage Foundation sought his US visa application to check statements about drug use, but the Department of Homeland Security refused to release it and a judge upheld that rejection. In Britain, his legal case against the Home Office drew questions about his truthfulness and some partners distanced themselves from him. Attempts at reconciliation with the royal family failed: William and Kate would not welcome Harry back, he was denied palace accommodation, and he did not meet William. Harry visited the Queen's grave and then attended the WellChild Awards, where he was warmly received as a longtime patron.

Arriving in Düsseldorf, Harry dominated the opening of the Invictus Games. About 500 competitors and 1,100 family members from 21 countries were present, and sponsors and organizers filled the stadium for a starry music show. After the buzzy opening, the mood turned flat: the vast Merkur Spiel-Arena sat mostly empty for the competitions, with few paying spectators in the large halls. Harry stayed mostly on the sidelines, because his team thought the athletes should be the focus. Meghan, however, seemed to have other plans.

On 12 September the tone changed when Meghan finally arrived. She apologized for being late, explaining she had to settle their children at home—"three milkshakes and a school drop-off"—and her short, practiced remarks captivated the 1,600 people who had turned up in a stadium built for 54,600. With Meghan center stage, Harry fell into the background; he looked noticeably sullen while she joked that he was "a man of few words."

Reports inside the couple’s circle described tension over the children and publicity. Harry wanted to keep their youngsters private and unphotographed for security and to avoid the exposure he experienced growing up. Meghan pushed the opposite view: she wanted their children visible and treated like normal Californian kids, eventually as public as William and Kate’s children. Witnesses said arguments followed and may have been patched up later in their hotel suite.

Meghan’s presence quickly became a fashion showcase. Her publicist briefed journalists repeatedly about what she wore: a black Banana Republic dress with a Bottega Veneta belt and Aquazzura heels, a J. Crew cardigan that sent the brand’s website into collapse, then Chanel, Givenchy, Zara, Celine and Saint Laurent. She posed for selfies and even promoted her stylist and colorist, whose salon she later invested in. Many delegates, including visitors from Vancouver, saw her looks as out of place at a wounded servicemen’s event and wondered why she did not wear sneakers.

Meghan also used the trip to highlight family ancestry, announcing DNA results that pointed to her mother’s Nigerian roots and waving a Nigerian flag beside Nigeria’s defense minister. She and the minister privately discussed a possible state visit. Meghan avoided interviews, but Harry wanted to be heard and agreed to work with German ZDF journalist Ulrike Grunewald, who followed them and reported they appeared close—"either that or very good actors." Harry relied on her to produce a favorable documentary.

To keep his profile, Harry’s thirty-ninth birthday was quietly leaked to Hello! magazine after a bierkeller dinner. The royal family did not mark the day, but Düsseldorf’s mayor brought a cake at a later stadium event, and Harry closed with an emotional speech praising the competitors: "You should not feel lost without your uniform," he told them, and repeated his thanks in German. He also took a sideways shot at British tabloids for ignoring veterans. After Düsseldorf the couple flew to Portugal to stay with Eugenie and Jack Brooksbank; Jack was working in property sales linked to George Clooney’s development, and rumors that the Sussexes bought a plot later surfaced to boost sales. Rejuvenated by the positive reception, the Sussexes returned to Montecito confident and set off three weeks later for another engagement.

On 10 October 2023, a convoy of seven SUVs escorted by police sirens moved Meghan and Harry from an Equinox Hotel in midtown Manhattan to Brooklyn, a sign of VVIP protection after a previous car chase. That morning they visited the alternative Marcy Lab School, where Meghan's outfit and accessories were publicized by her publicist. In a computer-room discussion about education she interrupted Harry and said, "I wish my kids were here with us but they are doing well." Back at the hotel she changed into a white Altuzarra top and expensive accessories before attending a meeting for parents whose children had committed suicide after online bullying.

The event was billed as the Archewell Foundation Parents Summit: Mental Wellness in a Digital Age. The Sussexes joined an intimate panel with NBC's Carson Daly and US Surgeon General Vivek Murthy to discuss how the internet affects youth mental health. Two families described suicides linked to social media. When Daly asked Harry what a healthy social media environment might look like, Harry replied, "I don't know." Meghan then spoke at length, moving the conversation to her own experiences. Harry said, inaccurately, that "There9s no one else working in this space," and Meghan steered discussion toward her alleged suicidal thoughts in Kensington Palace.

The fifty-minute panel drew only about 8,500 YouTube viewers and many hostile comments. Critics called Meghan9s remarks familiar and disappointing. Later that day the Sussexes flew by private jet to Canouan in the Grenadines on a plane owned by financier Ian Wace, a long-standing friend who had supported royal and charity events. Canouan is a discreet island popular with the very wealthy, with luxury amenities but reported social problems: restricted access for local fishermen, weak secondary schooling, poor healthcare and racial inequality among workers. Eyewitnesses said the couple seemed unconcerned as they moved between a villa, Soho House and empty beaches.

The island gave them space to regroup after a turbulent few months. Meghan focused on a comeback: Netflix agreed to Harry9s polo series and to produce Meghan9s lifestyle series tied to her American Riviera Orchard brand. Audible rejected Meghan9s podcast pitch, but Lemonada, a smaller company, signed her. They left Canouan confident they had commercial paths forward.

Harry then traveled alone to Austin to watch an F1 race and returned to Santa Barbara. Shortly afterward the cartoon Family Guy mocked them as entitled grifters sitting by a pool with a butler presenting cash. The stunt renewed criticism that they were profiting from fame. Meghan responded by appearing at high-profile events, including a Katy Perry concert in Las Vegas with other celebrities, though reports said Harry seemed bored and preoccupied by a dispute with his father.

Relations with the royal household remained strained. Conflicting reports circulated about Harry9s invitation to King Charles's seventy-fifth birthday; in fact he had not been invited. A private phone call between Harry and the King, their first in six months, was leaked to the press by Montecito publicists, irritating the Palace. Officials concluded the Sussexes could not be trusted and would not be welcomed at family gatherings while Harry9s litigation against the government continued. Tension intensified when People magazine serialized Omid Scobie9s book Endgame; what the Sussexes once welcomed as a chance to stir debate now felt poorly timed to them.

Omid Scobie became a trusted reporter for the Sussexes after they left Britain. He falsely denied that Meghan had helped him write Finding Freedom; in fact he had sent questions to the couple’s publicist and Meghan answered at length. After the couple moved to California, they and Scobie continued to rely on one another: he provided intimate accounts of their lives for American media, and they used his work to promote their version of events.

Scobie fit well with Meghan’s public image. He displayed a taste for luxury on social media, supported the couple in legal fights, and denounced critics. His loyalty went both ways: he acted as a public advocate and, according to his publishers and editors, received briefings from people close to the Sussexes.

After Finding Freedom, Scobie embraced the Sussexes’ narrative that the Royal Family was racist and hostile. He recast criticism of his first book and of himself as racially motivated, relocated to the U.S., and adopted a mixed-race identity. He and the Sussexes presented the Palace as an “entitled, exceedingly white” institution that pushed the couple away, a theme they shared and amplified together.

Scobie’s Endgame framed the monarchy as weakened, partly because the Sussexes left. HarperCollins and Scobie’s editors assumed Meghan had fed material for the book, especially the sections about Kate and allegations of racism. Endgame promised intimate and explosive details and was treated by Scobie and his publishers as an insider account of the family’s conflicts.

In the book Scobie portrayed Charles as cantankerous and indecisive, William as jealous and dishonest, and Kate as a jealous, Stepford-like future queen. He advanced Harry’s view that the family used dirty tricks, blamed Charles for some palace smear campaigns, and reported Harry’s denial of the 2021 bullying allegations. Scobie also softened Harry’s earlier harsh words about Camilla and included anecdotes meant to explain the early tangled relationships between Charles and Camilla.

Race was a central charge. Scobie reinforced Meghan’s claim in the Oprah interview that there were “several conversations . . . in those months when I was pregnant” about their unborn son’s skin color, and he stressed episodes like questions asked of Ngozi Fulani at a palace reception. Originally Scobie’s manuscript named Charles and Kate as the royals involved; after publisher objections he removed those names and said legal risks prevented identification.

In the weeks around Endgame’s release, the Sussexes and their team timed publicity carefully. Meghan staged a solo relaunch at Variety’s Power of Women gala while Harry posted a video of himself as William traveled to Singapore, moves that drew attention and suggested competition. The couple monitored the Cambridges’ public schedule and withheld some joint appearances to avoid damaging optics.

When Endgame appeared, Scobie claimed he had been briefed by insiders and defended the Sussexes. He denied any improper relationship with them when pressed about leaks, and he refused to explain why a letter from King Charles appeared to have been shared. Then the Dutch edition printed Charles’s and Kate’s names; translators said those names were in Scobie’s manuscript. After initial denials and excuses about translation, Scobie admitted an early, uncleared text included the names, and his credibility suffered.

Neither Harry nor Meghan publicly denounced Endgame or Scobie’s role. Public opinion saw the book as another crossing of a line. The double publications and the row over the manuscript closed the year with reconciliation looking unlikely; Endgame appeared to many as the Sussexes’ final, risky bid to set their version of events in stone.

The Hollywood Reporter labeled the Sussexes the "Biggest Losers" of 2023 after their criticized Netflix documentary and Prince Harry's memoir, saying their brand had collapsed. Critics blamed Shrabani Scobie and other reporting for creating a crisis of trust; commentators mocked the couple's mix of self-promotion, philanthropy, and anti-royal rhetoric, and even popular culture felt ready to puncture their image.

As the brand weakened, other celebrities and institutions distanced themselves. The Sussexes were not invited to the Duke of Westminster's wedding despite the groom being Archie's godfather, and Harry was left out of a Sandhurst volume of notable alumni that carried William's foreword. These snubs signaled a broader cooling of elite support.

Archewell, the Sussexes' foundation, showed sharp financial decline. Despite a polished promotional video, donations fell from £10.3 million to £1.6 million in 2022, coming from only two unnamed donors, and the charity posted an annual loss of £536,357. Only about $950,000 in gifts were recorded that year, including modest grants to gender justice work, flood relief, the Halo Trust, and a play space in Uvalde. Archewell paused many staff payments and kept consulting and PR fees in place, exposing a mismatch between aims and resources.

In 2023 Archewell reported recovery: $5.7 million raised, largely from Fidelity Charitable, but it paid out $1.3 million while incurring $1.9 million in expenses. The largest single donation that year was $250,000 to the Women’s Wellness Space in Philadelphia, a drop-in center run by Ashley Biden. That gift, and other payments, raised questions when Ashley's tax return showed total 2023 income of $170,296 despite receiving $500,000 in donations she did not report. The Heritage Foundation and some commentators suggested the Bidens had given the Sussexes special treatment, and Archewell's accounts themselves showed a puzzling discrepancy between declared income and the charity's records that the foundation did not fully explain.

Harry had mixed results in court. In his case against the Mirror, Judge Fancourt found "compelling evidence" that phone hacking had been widespread among editors, but he ruled Harry was a victim only to a modest extent, dismissing nearly half of his claims. After arguments over appropriate damages, the judge awarded £140,600 rather than the much larger sum Harry sought and criticized his lawyer for overreaching. Harry framed the ruling as a vindication and a defense of press freedom; critics such as Piers Morgan accused him of hypocrisy and of attacking the monarchy.

Legal fights ended unevenly. Harry continued litigation over many stories but ultimately accepted a settlement from the Mirror, whose insurers profited from the resolution. He also paid the Mail on Sunday £48,000 as part of a larger costs bill, which the newspaper described as an embarrassing defeat.

A separate legal battle over UK security failed disastrously. Harry sought taxpayer-funded armed protection for visits to Britain, arguing he and his family faced assassination risk and stressing the UK was central to his children. Agencies and police found no credible ongoing threat and described a system of case-by-case assessments as appropriate. Harry pressed the issue by lobbying U.S. officials after a New York car chase, asking California and Manhattan prosecutors to pursue photographers; U.S. prosecutors found his interventions heavy-handed and ineffective. A new letter from a New York intelligence chief that appeared to reverse an earlier view was treated with skepticism and led to no prosecutions.

Judge Peter Lane rejected Harry's legal claim about security, finding the Home Office's bespoke arrangements lawful, and ordered Harry to pay 90 percent of the government's costs, about £514,000; his own legal fees were roughly £1 million and he had no right to appeal. After losing, Harry sent confidential security memoranda, which were protected under the Official Secrets Act, to MP Johnny Mercer. That act of disclosure suggested, to critics, a willingness to flout rules and an attitude of being above the law.

Harry was actively seeking chances to present himself favorably when he accepted the Living Legend of Aviation award at the Beverly Hilton on January 19, 2024. Organizers hoped Meghan would attend; she did not. John Travolta presented the prize. The program listed Harry as a pilot in Afghanistan, though he had served as an Apache gunner and co-pilot. Critics, including Admiral Lord West, scoffed that calling him a “living legend of aviation” was pathetic.

Also honored that night was Lauren Sanchez, Jeff Bezos’s partner, celebrated for qualifying as a helicopter pilot. Her presence and wealth guaranteed media coverage and underscored the contrast with the Sussexes’ limited resources. Meghan’s absence from the event, explained as “caring for a sick child,” looked tactical. Four days later the Sussexes were not invited to Bezos’s sixtieth birthday party, despite many A-list neighbors attending, which disappointed Meghan.

At the ceremony Sanchez posted a photo with Harry. He also posed with local royal figure Prince Mario-Max Schaumburg-Lippe and joked to Travolta about being one when his mother danced with the actor. He did not acknowledge the big news that day: both King Charles and Kate had been admitted to the London Clinic for surgery. William noted Harry’s silence. Harry had been seen leaving a gym earlier, an appearance some felt was staged to be noticed.

Ignoring the Bezos snub, the Sussexes soon flew to Jamaica for the premiere of One Love, a Bob Marley biopic, invited by Paramount CEO Brian Robbins. They arrived with Robbins from Montecito to join the after-party VIP group. Harry stood beside Jamaica’s prime minister, Andrew Holness, despite Holness’s previous public criticism of the royal family and his move to remove the King as head of state. The couple showed little concern about those political implications.

Paramount’s publicists had calculated the premiere and after-party would be ignored without the Sussexes. Meghan displayed Hollywood glamour in a black Carolina Herrera outfit and high-end accessories. The British High Commissioner observed but kept a low profile, worried the couple’s appearance could be read as endorsing Holness’s stance. Press attention also revived old gossip about Meghan’s earlier visit to Jamaica.

Back in California the Sussexes faced staffing turbulence: another Archewell executive resigned, the fifth departure in months, signaling trouble at the operation. At the Golden Globes the couple were mocked for being paid large sums for little visible work. They were promoting upcoming projects, but momentum looked thin. In response, Meghan and Harry rebranded their commercial site as Sussex.com and styled it “The Office of Prince Harry and Meghan,” using a royal coat of arms despite Sandringham rules against trading on royal status.

The new site cast Meghan as one of the world’s most influential women and Harry as a humanitarian and veteran. Archewell’s language shifted toward business and brand building, not just charity: “Shaping the future through business and philanthropy.” Ahead of the relaunch they issued statements on child wellbeing and online abuse, and both used personal grief in public remarks, with Meghan invoking visits to tragedies such as Uvalde. The chapter closes with the note that more bad news awaited the family back in Britain.

On 5 February, news broke that King Charles had early-stage prostate cancer. Doctors said his prognosis was positive, but he stopped public duties and moved to Sandringham for weekly treatment. Under the revised Regency Act, Prince Edward and Princess Anne could act for him; Prince Harry was excluded. Insiders said the King became fragile and depressed, and some worried his early death would force a premature succession to William, who they judged unready.

Senior courtiers questioned William’s grasp of monarchy traditions, ritual, and history. They were alarmed by his talk of a shorter, less symbolic coronation and by the absence of a seasoned counselor to teach him how to treat the Crown as an institution. Critics described his lifestyle as too casual for a future monarch and feared he lacked firm guiding figures like the late advisers Martin Charteris or Christopher Geidt.

Hours after the cancer news, Harry phoned Charles and flew to London for a brief meeting at Clarence House. Camilla and advisers had warned that a tense, public confrontation must be avoided. Charles kept details of his illness private and limited their conversation to thirty minutes. Palace insiders expected Harry might demand armed protection, money, or status rather than offer a genuine apology or reconciliation.

After the meeting Harry returned to Los Angeles and that night presented a paid appearance in Las Vegas, giving a sports award and joking briefly about American football. Observers asked whether his London trip was primarily to secure material for media projects. Harry later said on ABC that he loved his family and could always get on a plane to see them.

Buckingham Palace responded by downgrading the Sussexes’ biographies on the official website and labeling them non-working royals. Open tensions resumed. Two weeks later the couple flew to Vancouver to inspect 2025 Invictus Games preparations; Meghan promoted a Netflix series on the same trip. The Invictus Games remained central to Harry’s public profile.

Life in Montecito felt increasingly solitary for Harry. Friends said he was drifting, with Netflix projects and occasional polo replacing a clear sense of purpose. A Netflix polo series was announced that spring.

US politics added pressure. After Harry’s criticism of Donald Trump, Trump and his son Eric publicly attacked the Sussexes. Trump suggested he would not extend special protection to Harry if elected and spoke of consequences for what he called Harry’s betrayal of the late Queen. Harry said he had considered American citizenship but it was not a priority. Meanwhile, the Biden administration shielded Harry from efforts to release his visa application.

At a late-February memorial for King Constantine of Greece, the family’s public image suffered more wounds. William was absent at short notice because Kate had been diagnosed with cancer, and news of Tom Kingston’s suicide, linked to business debts, reached the family on the drive to Windsor. During the procession some senior royals pushed forward for the cameras, prompting media claims that the Palace had mishandled the moment.

The monarchy’s troubles continued: the King missed the Commonwealth Day service because of his illness, and then Kate’s office posted a Mother’s Day photo that a photo agency revealed had been digitally altered. The discovery sparked global rumors and accusations about cover-ups, William’s behavior, and succession changes. Palace advisors lost control of the narrative as online speculation swirled.

Shortly after, Kate released a video confirming her cancer. Public reaction mixed sympathy with derision from some quarters. At the same time, examples of digital editing involving Meghan and Harry—such as a retouched cover photo and a previously altered image of Meghan—reentered the debate. Meghan spoke on CBS about suicidal thoughts inside Kensington Palace and later criticized female media executives for allowing online bullying, without mentioning Kate; critics noted she and her allies had sometimes fed negative stories about Kate.

To repair her image in Britain, Meghan hired British publicist Charlie Gipson. Harry reportedly sent a message of support to William and Kate, saying he wished them health and healing, but the Sussexes then confirmed that message to People magazine, undermining its privacy. The episode highlighted the contradictions and continuing public battles in the Sussexes’ fragile relationship with the Royal Family.

Nothing was ready but Meghan was eager. After Canouan she planned a Netflix series called With Love, Meghan to present herself as a self-made star and homemaker-entrepreneur. Netflix’s head of content, Bela Bajaria, had bought the idea and Archewell was named as producer, but executives knew Meghan needed help turning fame into a workable lifestyle brand. Her pitch emphasized accessible style, “high-low” fashion, family pleasures and financial literacy for women, framing the project as sincere and not trading on royal status. Yet turning that vision into a real business required products, design and distribution partners and capital she did not yet have.

Netflix recruited experienced reality producers from Sony and director Michael Steed, vetted a crew of more than seventy and required NDAs. The production plan had Meghan hosting in a Montecito kitchen, combining gardening, crafting and entertaining, but she could not supply original recipes or reliable product ideas. She admitted she enjoyed making jam but could only manage simple cooking, so the show would feature guest chefs and focus on presentation rather than culinary invention. Production relied on what Meghan provided: styling, staging and a curated domestic image more than technical or culinary expertise.

Commercial plans stalled. Meghan’s Delaware company, Mama Knows Best, had a product line declared but no chief executive and little operational structure; suitable candidates did not step forward. Under time pressure she prepared to launch American Riviera Orchard, her consumer brand, prematurely. She arranged fifty jars of strawberry jam claimed to be homemade from her garden crop. Independent jam makers estimated that amount of jam would require at least 100 pounds of strawberries, far more than Meghan’s beds appeared to yield. The jam was thin and berry-sparse; suppliers and the jar maker declined to be identified.

A week before the official trailer, the fifty jars were sent to celebrity friends and influencers, each asked to praise the product on social media, sometimes with scripted snippets from Meghan’s publicists. Abigail Spencer’s staged photo on the grass with a pot of jam and lemons gained attention while Meghan posted a short Instagram video. The trailer showed Meghan in a white tank top and a black ballgown arranging flowers, whisking, drizzling oil and plating small bites to Nancy Wilson’s song; the caption read, “Everybody’s invited to create wonder in every moment.” The visuals emphasized hosting, hospitality and the image of the American Dream more than original cooking.

The March social drop coincided with other publicity moves. On the same evening William greeted Diana Legacy Award winners in London, Archewell announced $100,000 civil rights awards, and Netflix prepared to unveil two Sussex series. The timing was criticized as self-promotion and led to questions about Meghan’s lifestyle claims: large homes, expensive cookware, a rented nearby farm growing cannabis, and why filming and product launches were rushed. Trolls and critical commentary followed, and Meghan privately reacted with distress while publicly maintaining a composed image.

Netflix advanced the announcement to April 14 and the couple sought further publicity at a Sentebale charity polo match in Palm Beach on April 12. Sentebale was already in turmoil: Harry had called the charity an “albatross” earlier that year, pushed for leadership changes and relocation, and backed a plan to film the polo match for his own Netflix series. The original venue declined to allow commercial filming; a friend offered an alternative but at great cost and strain on organisers and donors. Meghan’s presence provoked worry among event staff and sponsors that some would withdraw.

At the match Meghan arrived dressed elaborately, accompanied by Serena Williams. Her demands in the marquee unsettled local staff and moved staff to tears. On the podium she intervened in the presentation sequence: when Sophie Chandauka expected to present the cup, film direction and Meghan’s insistence displaced Chandauka while Meghan took the kiss-and-pose moment with Harry. Video of the incident circulated and drew criticism. Harry asked staff to persuade Chandauka to issue a rebuttal to online attacks; Chandauka resisted, refusing to be used to deflect criticism of a black woman. A tense phone call followed and she thought an accommodation had been reached, but the episode left Sentebale strained and relationships unsettled.

Harry smiled as he climbed the steps to St Paul’s Cathedral on 8 May 2024 for the tenth-anniversary Invictus service. The event was staged to announce the Sussexes’ status and to rival the King’s Buckingham Palace garden party, held at a moment when the King had just revealed his cancer diagnosis. Many saw the St Paul’s appearance as a deliberate bid to assert the Montecito couple’s place in the royal story.

Media attention surrounded whether Harry would see his father. Palace spokespeople and Harry offered different accounts; Harry said he had been refused, while the Palace said he had turned down an offered bedroom in a royal residence on security grounds. Harry said leaving a palace without police protection would be dangerous, so he stayed at Coworth Park Hotel instead.

The Invictus Games were central to Harry’s identity. The event, backed by militaries in more than twenty countries, raised about $60 million and offered real benefits to injured service members; at an estimated $120,000 per competitor, supporters argued it was a meaningful investment. Yet Harry’s wider media projects fared poorly: his Netflix series Heart of Invictus drew fewer than 300,000 viewers since August 2023, a tiny fraction of mainstream hits.

Public opinion favored the King and Catherine over the Sussexes. The King’s approval rose while Kate’s remained high and Meghan’s rating lagged. Family tensions surfaced in small moments: Charles Spencer refused a request to let Archie and Lilibet change their surname to Spencer, and Harry read about service at St Paul’s while members of his extended family watched, underlining disconnects rather than reconciliation.

Harry met Meghan after the service and the couple flew to Nigeria, aiming for what they hoped would be a globally visible, quasi-royal tour. They arrived in Abuja with an armed military welcome; thirty-one suitcases were loaded onto an army truck, handlers and bodyguards were present, and Josh Kettler, newly hired as Harry’s chief of staff, led their team. A tight, security-heavy convoy took them through the capital amid excited onlookers.

Behind the public narrative, the trip was planned as much for the Sussexes’ self-promotion as for Invictus. David Wiseman, Invictus’s director, and Air Vice-Marshal Abidemi Timothy Marquis worked for months to bring them. Nigeria’s defence chief, General Christopher Musa, embraced Invictus as a morale tool in the fight against Boko Haram, and many Nigerians were predisposed to support Meghan after her acting career, her Oprah interview, and publicized ancestry tests showing Nigerian roots.

The Sussexes tightly controlled the itinerary and the media, excluding most journalists and inviting only lifestyle outlets like Harper’s Bazaar and People. The emphasis on curated access ensured coverage focused on warm welcomes and Meghan’s fashion. British officials quietly warned Nigeria’s leaders that London would prefer the couple not be publicly hosted by the president, but the hosts welcomed them enthusiastically.

At a visit to Lightway Academy in Abuja, Meghan spoke warmly to students, calling Nigeria "my country, my home" and repeatedly relating herself to the audience. Later in Kaduna, Harry met wounded soldiers, spoke about resilience, and joined a wheelchair basketball game, urging rehabilitation through sport with lines like "Get better, be strong." The meetings energized troops and highlighted Invictus’s promise, even as the organization faced a longer-term problem: many founding nations were not fighting wars, so recruiting countries with recent combat casualties became critical.

Public receptions continued, with Meghan receiving traditional gifts and a Yoruba name at a defense headquarters event and speaking on panels about women’s empowerment. Some local figures criticized her dress choices; palace officials back home fumed that the couple’s tone suggested they still acted like fully funded working royals. At private meals, hosts echoed Meghan’s account of a conversation about race within the royal family. Harry did not correct every detail but declared, "She was murdered," when Diana’s death came up, and Nigerian officers affirmed the couple’s narrative of Palace mistreatment.

The visit also advanced ambitions for future Invictus events: Nigeria expressed interest in bidding for 2029 while Harry hinted Meghan would attend Birmingham events. Yet the trip exposed contrasts: Meghan’s frequent designer outfits and staged moments sat uneasily against Nigeria’s deep economic problems, and most national papers barely covered the visit. In Lagos, the couple flew on a private Air Peace plane owned by Allen Onyema, a businessman later revealed to face a U.S. fraud indictment; the Sussexes nonetheless accepted his hospitality and moved through a busy schedule of schools, sports, and a governor’s reception.

Archewell’s public pledges proved limited in cash terms: accounts later showed only $10,000 committed in Nigeria. The Sussexes returned to Los Angeles planning further trips. Commentators noted a nostalgic appeal in parts of the visit, seeing Harry and Meghan’s charisma on display, even as critics pointed to the staging, the political risks, and lingering questions about motives, money, and messaging.

ESPN gave Harry the Pat Tillman Award at a Los Angeles ceremony on July 11, 2024, an honor meant for people who show courage, perseverance and service. The prize, created by the family of NFL player Pat Tillman, is a high-profile recognition of humanitarian work and felt like a sporting equivalent of the Oscars.

Pat Tillman had left a lucrative NFL career to enlist after 9/11 and was killed by friendly fire in Afghanistan. His widow and mother helped establish the award; ESPN executive Kate Jackson called Harry one of the ‘‘incredible individuals’’ who change the world. Mary Tillman, not told in advance of Harry’s nomination, publicly denounced the choice, saying he had abandoned duty for fame and was unworthy of the award.

Mary Tillman’s complaint sparked a fast backlash: a petition grew from 12,000 to 68,000 signatures in ten days. Harry’s office said the reaction ‘‘took the shine off the award.’’ Still, no one doubted his charity work; as patron of Scotty’s Little Soldiers he supported hundreds of bereaved children and told them their lost parent ‘‘wants you to be happy.’’

Mary Tillman did not attend the Dolby Theatre. Serena Williams hosted and Meghan appeared in the official photograph, which fed speculation that their joint agents influenced the award. Harry, visibly nervous, read a prepared speech praising the Invictus Games for uniting injured veterans, but applause could not hide the wider controversy: polls showed Meghan and Harry with very low approval in Britain and divided opinion in the U.S.

Harry’s other public appearances suffered similar problems. At a BetterUp conference in San Francisco he spoke about ‘‘building resilience’’ while the company faced public complaints from a hundred ex-employees about a toxic workplace. The event, with $1,500 tickets, was overshadowed by those accusations.

Netflix also had doubts about a planned polo series. Executives and producers said viewers had no connection to polo or its wealthy players. The original plan promised close access to Harry and Meghan socializing with the polo set, but Harry refused to play matches on camera. Meghan disliked polo and horses, and Harry feared being linked to racy playboys, so the series shifted focus toward polo star Nacho Figueras.

Harry’s relationship with his family remained strained. He continued his appeal in Britain for armed protection and repeatedly asked Palace officials for a meeting with his father; officials said the king was unavailable. On Father’s Day William posted a message to Charles while Harry stayed silent. He was also sidelined socially, blacklisted from some events, and angered by public comments from figures like Jeremy Clarkson.

Harry blamed the tabloids for damaging his family ties and pursued legal action, focusing on News UK and its former editor and chief executive Rebekah Brooks. He remembered meeting Brooks and feeling ‘‘duped’’ by her charm. News UK had closed the News of the World after phone-hacking scandals, paid many settlements without admitting liability, and faced renewed accusations from Harry and other claimants of unlawful newsgathering, allegations the company denied.

The litigation was costly and contentious. By April 2024 estimated legal bills for claimants exceeded £10 million and were expected to rise toward £30 million. Many original claimants dropped out after settlements; Hugh Grant accepted a large payment through insurers. Harry and a few others, including the peer Tom Watson, continued. Harry said he would press on for accountability even if the payout were far smaller than offers to settle.

Judge Timothy Fancourt criticized Harry’s legal team for making unproven allegations and for failures to preserve and disclose evidence. Harry admitted wiping his chat history with a writer and did not hand over some documents from Buckingham Palace, behavior the judge called troubling and obstructive. Harry was ordered to explain his conduct in a witness statement and to pay £60,000 in costs; some claims were withdrawn by his lawyers after failing to produce proof.

Meanwhile Meghan worried the courtroom battles were harming her own projects. In late July she flew to the Hamptons with cosmetics founder Jamie Kern Lima and attended a party hosted by Amy Griffin with guests such as Sara Blakely and Gwyneth Paltrow. Meghan sent Lima a basket bearing a card signed ‘‘HRH Duchess of Sussex,’’ which outraged observers in London for seeming to contravene an agreement about using the HRH title; Meghan’s spokesman denied she had used the title.

In spring 2024 Meghan received an unexpected invitation from Colombia's vice president, Francia Marquez, for a four-day visit. Marquez, a high-profile left-wing figure and descendant of African slaves, told Meghan she felt a kinship after watching Harry & Meghan and wanted to “build bridges, open doors and address a problem which concerns all of humanity.” Known for pushing reparations and criticizing colonial powers, Marquez assumed Meghan shared her anti-imperialist views and expected a large Archewell donation at the visit's end. Meghan accepted the platform without publicly probing Marquez's motives.

Marquez's own story is striking: she rose from sleeping on a dirt floor and working in a gold mine to vice president. But at home she faced accusations of corruption, had received death threats, and presided over a country where poverty rose from 30.9% in 2016 to 34.5% in 2022 and extreme poverty from 12% to 16.9%. Critics said she had used public funds to build a private house. The visit offered her publicity and political cover; for Meghan it offered a global stage.

Meghan sent three senior Archewell staff to Bogotá to plan details: chief of staff Miranda Barbot, publicity chief Ashley Hansen, and co-executive director Shauna Nep. They met diplomat Andrea Alfonso and lawyer Tatiana Escobar from Marquez's office. The Americans did not challenge the proposed events; they focused on security and branding instead. Meghan planned to treat the trip as a fashion and media opportunity while the Colombians expected political and philanthropic support.

Security and media controls were strict. To protect the Sussexes there would be no advance notice of the visit, venues would learn identities only shortly before arrival, and foreign journalists were excluded except for Harper's Bazaar columnist Bianca Betancourt and Archewell photographer Eric Charbonneau. Betancourt was asked to emphasize Meghan's fashion. Branding was central: Meghan had already arranged Bazaar and New York Times exposure for her post-London 'freedom style.'
